title: Case 89430: College Decision 058/26/COL - Norway - Methodology for sharing Firmness and Remuneration Costs (FRC)
text:
Case No: 89430
Document No: 1584796
Decision No: 058/26/COL
EFTA SURVEILLANCE AUTHORITY DECISION
of 18 March 2026
on the methodology for sharing Firmness and Remuneration Costs (FRC) of long-
term transmission rights for Norway
THE EFTA SURVEILLANCE AUTHORITY,
Having regard to:
the Agreement on the European Economic Area1 (‘the EEA Agreement’), in particular to
Article 24 and Annex IV thereof,
the Act referred to at point 47 of Annex IV to the EEA Agreement:
Regulation (EC) No 713/2009 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 13
July 2009 establishing an Agency for the Cooperation of Energy Regulators (‘the
ACER Regulation’),2 and in particular Article 8 thereof, as adapted to the EEA
Agreement,3
the procedure referred to at point 47(d) of Annex IV to the EEA Agreement,
the Act referred to at point 50 of Annex IV to the EEA Agreement:
Commission Regulation (EU) 2016/1719 of 26 September 2016 establishing a
guideline on forward capacity allocation (‘FCA Regulation’),4 and in particular
Articles 4(6)(g) and 61 thereof, as adapted to the EEA Agreement,5
Whereas:
1 OJ L 1, 3.1.1994, p. 3.
2 OJ L 211, 14.8.2009, p. 1.
3 EEA Joint Committee Decision No. 93/2017 of 5 May 2017.
4 OJ L 259, 27.9.2016, p. 42.
5 EEA Joint Committee Decision No. 205/2020 of 11 December 2020.
Avenue des Arts 19H, 1000 Brussels, tel: +32 2 286 18 11, www.eftasurv.int

Page 2
1 INTRODUCTION
(1) The FCA Regulation lays down a range of requirements for the internal electricity
market, amongst them requirements for cross-zonal capacity allocation in the forward
markets. These requirements include requirements for a methodology for sharing
costs incurred to ensure firmness and remuneration of long-term transmission rights
(‘LTTRs’), developed by all transmission system operators (‘TSOs’) in accordance
with Article 61 of the FCA Regulation (‘FRC methodology’).
(2) Norway is part of the internal market for electricity through the EEA Agreement. The
objective of the EEA Agreement is to create a homogeneous European Economic
Area (‘EEA’). Relevant EU legislation for the internal electricity market is incorporated
into the EEA Agreement so that it applies throughout the whole EEA, ensuring
uniform application of laws relating to this market and thus ensuring a level playing
field throughout the EEA. Upon incorporation of the EU legislation into the EEA
Agreement, Norway has the obligation to implement the acquis covered by this
legislation.
(3) The FCA Regulation was incorporated into the EEA Agreement by Decision of the
EEA Joint Committee 205/2020, which entered into force on 31 July 2021.6 It is
therefore appropriate to apply the FCA Regulation’s requirements on the FRC
methodology to Norway.
(4) The present Decision follows from the request of the Norwegian regulatory authority
(‘RME’) that the EFTA Surveillance Authority (‘the Authority’) adopts a decision for
Norway on the proposal it received from the European Network of Transmission
System Operators for Electricity (‘ENTSO-E’) on behalf of the Norwegian TSO,
Statnett, for the FRC methodology for Norway (‘the Proposal’).
(5) The present Decision includes the following annex:
Annex I to Decision No. 058/26/COL of 18 March 2026 on the methodology
for sharing costs incurred to ensure firmness and remuneration of
long-term transmission rights (FRC) for Norway in accordance with
Article 61 of Commission Regulation (EU) 2016/1719 establishing a
guideline on forward capacity allocation, determining the
methodology for the sharing of costs incurred to ensure firmness and
remuneration of long-term transmission rights for Norway, as
amended and approved by the EFTA Surveillance Authority
(Document No. 1586064).
For information purposes only, the Authority’s Decision is accompanied by a text
providing a track-changed version of the Proposal, reflecting the Authority’s
amendments (Document No. 1589529).
2 PROCEDURE
(6) On 20 October 2022, ENTSO-E on behalf of the Norwegian TSO, Statnett, submitted
the Proposal to RME.
(7) RME requested on 3 March 2023 (Document No. 1357183) the Authority to decide
on the Proposal in accordance with point 47 of Annex IV to the EEA Agreement, in
particular the adaptations set out in paragraph (d) thereof for the application of Article
8(1)(b) of the ACER Regulation, as incorporated into the EEA Agreement by Decision
of the EEA Joint Committee No. 93/2017, and Article 4(10) of the FCA, as
incorporated into the EEA Agreement by Decision of the EEA Joint Committee No.
205/2020 and having regard in particular to paragraph (9) of the preamble thereof.
6 The FCA Regulation was made binding in the internal legal order in Norway through national
measures, which entered into force on 1 August 2021.

Page 3
(8) On 3 July 2023, the Authority requested the European Union Agency for the
Cooperation of Energy Regulators (‘ACER’) to prepare a draft for the Authority’s
decision on the Proposal in accordance with point 47(d)(iv) of Annex IV to the EEA
Agreement (Document No. 1384919).
(9) On 12 December 2023, ACER provided the Authority with a draft for the Authority’s
decision on the Proposal, in accordance with point 47(d)(iv) of Annex IV to the EEA
Agreement (Documents No. 1594866, 1594865, 1594864, 1594863 and 1594862).
(10) On 10 February 2026, the Authority invited RME to express its views on the
Authority’s preliminary decision, in accordance with point 47(d)(iv) of Annex IV to the
EEA Agreement (Documents No. 1593395, 1593396, 1593397 and 1593398).
(11) On 26 February 2026, RME shared its views on the Authority’s preliminary decision
(Document No. 1592908). These views are summarised in section 5 of this Decision.
(12) This Decision is issued following the Authority’s review and amendment of the
Proposal, in cooperation with ACER. Annex I to this Decision sets out the FRC
methodology for Norway, pursuant to Articles 4(6)(g) and 61 of the FCA Regulation,
as amended and approved by the Authority.
(13) The Authority’s Decision determines the FRC methodology solely for Norway.
• For the purposes of this Decision, it is relevant to note that the FRC
methodology for the EU has been approved by ACER decisions, namely by
decisions of:
o 23 October 2020 (‘ACER Decision No. 25/2020’);
o 4 October 2021, replacing ACER Decision No. 25/2020 after an
appeal and remittal by ACER’s Board of Appeal (‘ACER Decision No.
12/2021’);7 and
o 22 March 2023, amending ACER Decision No. 12/2021 on the basis
of the proposal received from the EU TSOs (‘the EU Proposal’)
(‘ACER Decision No. 07/2023’).8
7 ACER Decision No. 12/2021 of 4 October 2021 on sharing costs incurred to ensure firmness and
remuneration of long-term transmission rights. ACER Decision No. 12/2021 is available under this
link:
https://www.acer.europa.eu/sites/default/files/documents/Individual%20Decisions/ACER%20Decisi
on%2012-2021%20on%20the%20FCA-FRC%20Methodology_0.pdf.
Annex I to Decision No. 12/2021 is available under this link:
https://www.acer.europa.eu/sites/default/files/documents/Individual%20Decisions_annex/ACER%2
0Decision%20on%20the%20FCA-FRC%20Methodology%20-%20Annex%20I_0.pdf.
Annex Ia to Decision No. 12/2021 is available under this link:
https://www.acer.europa.eu/sites/default/files/documents/Individual%20Decisions_annex/ACER%2
0Decision%20on%20the%20FCA-FRC%20Methodology%20-%20Annex%20Ia_0.pdf.
Annex II to Decision No. 12/2021 is available under this link:
https://www.acer.europa.eu/sites/default/files/documents/Individual%20Decisions_annex/ACER%2
0Decision%20on%20the%20FCA-FRC%20Methodology%20-%20Annex%20II_0.pdf.
8 ACER Decision No. 07/2023 of 22 March 2023 is approving amendments to the methodology for
sharing costs incurred to ensure firmness and remuneration of long-term transmission rights. ACER
Decision No. 07/2023 is available under this link:
https://www.acer.europa.eu/sites/default/files/documents/Individual%20Decisions/ACER_Decision
_07-2023_on_FRC.pdf.
Annex I to Decision No. 07/2023 is available under this link:
https://www.acer.europa.eu/sites/default/files/documents/Individual%20Decisions_annex/ACER_D
ecision_07-2023_on_FRC_Annex_I.pdf.
Annex Ia to Decision No. 07/2023 is available under this link:
https://www.acer.europa.eu/sites/default/files/documents/Individual%20Decisions_annex/ACER_D
ecision_07-2023_on_FRC_Annex_Ia.pdf.

Page 4
(14) ACER, when taking its decisions for the EU, held numerous dialogues and
exchanges with the relevant EEA TSOs and national regulatory authorities. The
dialogues and exchanges are documented in detail in ACER’s Decisions.
(15) The content of the Proposal, received by the Authority from RME, is aligned on the
EU Proposal’s content, upon which ACER decided for the EU by Decision No.
07/2023.
3 THE AUTHORITY’S COMPETENCE TO DECIDE ON THE PROPOSAL
(16) In accordance with point 47(d)(i) of Annex IV to the EEA Agreement, adapting Article
8 of the ACER Regulation, in cases involving one or more EFTA State(s), the
Authority is to adopt a decision addressed to the national regulatory authorities of the
concerned EFTA State(s), where ACER is competent on the EU side.
(17) On 3 March 2023, RME referred the Proposal to the Authority for a decision in
accordance with point 47(d)(i) of Annex IV to the EEA Agreement, adapting Article
8(1)(b) of the ACER Regulation.
(18) The Proposal’s content falls within the scope of Article 61 of the FCA Regulation. The
FCA Regulation was incorporated at point 50 of Annex IV to the EEA Agreement by
Decision of the EEA Joint Committee No. 205/2020, which entered into force on 31
July 2021.
(19) Following the incorporation of the FCA Regulation into the EEA Agreement, the FRC
methodology involves one EFTA State, namely Norway. In the EU, ACER decided
on the corresponding proposal through binding decisions.
(20) Consequently, it is the Authority’s competence to adopt a decision addressed to RME
to determine the FRC methodology for Norway.
4 SUMMARY OF THE PROPOSAL
(21) The Proposal includes the following elements:
(a) the ‘Whereas’ section and Title 1 containing Articles 1 and 2, which include the
subject matter and scope as well as definitions and interpretation;
(b) Title 2, covering the specification of the cost sharing remuneration, which
includes Articles 3, 4 and 5 that specify for eligible LTTRs among bidding zone
borders (‘BZBs’) of Long-Term net transmission capacity (‘NTC’) capacity
calculation regions (‘CCRs’), BZBs of Long-Term Flow-based CCRs and TSOs
on a BZB;
(c) Title 3, covering sharing of compensation costs, which includes Article 6 that
determines the cost sharing due to curtailment of LTTRs;
(d) Title 4, covering final provisions in Articles 7 and 8 on the publication,
implementation and revision of the FRC methodology and the language.
Annex II to Decision No. 07/2023 is available under this link:
https://www.acer.europa.eu/sites/default/files/documents/Individual%20Decisions_annex/ACER_D
ecision_07-2023_on_FRC%20_Annex_II.pdf.

Page 5
5 OBSERVATIONS RECEIVED BY THE AUTHORITY
(22) RME observed that the Authority’s preliminary decision supported the overall
efficiency of long-term capacity allocation within the EEA in line with Article 61 of the
FCA Regulation.
(23) RME further considered that the methodology as annexed to the Authority’s
preliminary decision supported the objectives of the FCA Regulation as outlined in
Article 3 of the FCA Regulation by providing clear, objective and non-discriminatory
rules for sharing the costs of firmness and remuneration of LTTRs. It considered that
the methodology contributed to effective long-term cross-zonal trade, efficient
capacity allocation and orderly price formation. RME also noted that the clear rules
enhanced transparency and in sum supported efficient market functioning and
system development.
(24) RME especially welcomed the adaptations made by the Authority to reflect the
relevant legal framework of the FCA Regulation and the Third Energy Package, as
incorporated into the EEA Agreement. RME considered that these adaptations made
were not only necessary, but appropriate and well-balanced against the need to
ensure a homogeneous approach, the latter being essential to ensure the functioning
of the internal market.
(25) RME found that the content of the preliminary decision fell within the scope of Article
61 of the FCA Regulation. Furthermore, RME found that the FCA Regulation and
Regulation (EC) No. 714/2009 of 13 July 2009 on conditions for access to the network
for cross-border exchanges in electricity and repealing Regulation (EC) No
1228/20039 provided the relevant framework for the determination of the FRC
methodology for Norway as set out in the Authority’s preliminary decision.
6 ASSESSMENT OF THE PROPOSAL
6.1. Legal framework
(26) Article 61 of the FCA Regulation sets out specific requirements for the FRC
methodology.
(27) In particular, according to Article 61(1) of the FCA Regulation, the cost of ensuring
firmness shall include costs incurred from compensation mechanisms associated
with ensuring firmness of cross-zonal capacities as well as the cost of re-dispatching,
countertrading and imbalance associated with compensating market participants and
be borne by TSOs. In addition, according to Article 61(3) of the FCA Regulation, the
FRC methodology shall be consistent with the methodology for congestion income
distribution (‘FCA-CID methodology’)10 under Article 57 of the same Regulation.
(28) Article 4(8) of the FCA Regulation requires proposals to include a proposed timescale
for its implementation and a description of the expected impact on the objectives of
the FCA Regulation.
(29) Article 30(7) of the FCA Regulation provides that Article 61 of the same Regulation
shall not apply to the TSOs of the bidding zone borders in the event that regulatory
9 OJ L 211, 14.8.2009, p. 15, as incorporated at point 20 of Annex IV to the EEA Agreement by
Decision of the EEA Joint Committee No. 93/2017 of 5 May 2017.
10 EFTA Surveillance Authority Decision No. 201/2025/COL of 3 December 2025 on the forward
capacity allocation congestion income distribution methodology for Norway. The Decision can be
found under this link:
https://www.eftasurv.int/cms/sites/default/files/documents/gopro/Case%2089429%20College%20D
ecision%20201%2025%20COL%20-%20Norway%20-
%20Decision%20on%20the%20congestion%20income%20distribution%20%28CID%29%20meth
odology.pdf.

Page 6
authorities decide that LTTRs shall not be issued by the respective TSOs or that other
long-term cross-zonal hedging products shall be made available by the respective
TSOs.
(30) As outlined in section 1 of this Decision, Article 1 of the EEA Agreement defines the
aim of the EEA Agreement as achieving a homogeneous EEA based on common
rules and equal conditions of competition.
(31) The Decisions of the EEA Joint Committee No. 93/2017 and No. 205/2020 set up a
mechanism catering for a homogeneous interpretation and application of the ACER
Regulation and the FCA Regulation throughout the EEA. Under these acts, the
Authority is the competent authority to issue binding decisions vis-à-vis the
concerned national regulatory authorities of the EFTA States in cases involving one
or more EFTA State(s), where ACER is the competent authority on the EU side. The
decisions of the Authority are to be based on drafts prepared by ACER. The Authority
is to invite the national regulatory authorities of the EFTA States to express their
views on the matter, taking full account of the urgency, complexity and potential
consequences of the matter.
6.2. Assessment of the legal requirements
(32) The Proposal provides the FRC methodology for Norway.
(33) Considering the objective of the EEA Agreement of achieving a homogeneous EEA
based on common rules, as emphasised in sections 1 and 6.1 of this Decision, the
Authority is to adopt decisions consistent with decisions taken by ACER on similar
matters.
(34) The essence of the FCA Regulation and the FRC methodology calls for a
homogeneous approach. This is needed to be able to reach the objective of
optimising the forward capacity allocation of the transmission system. The FRC
methodology will promote this objective, by laying down objective criteria and
solutions for the sharing of costs to be applied by all involved TSOs. The FCA
Regulation through the FRC methodology establishes rules on how LTTRs curtailed
before the day-ahead firmness deadline should be reimbursed or compensated by
TSOs to the holders of LTTRs. Having homogeneous rules throughout the EEA is
crucial for optimising the calculation and allocation of long-term cross-zonal capacity,
which is necessary for an efficient use of the interconnected grid and to ensure
fairness for market participants.
(35) The Authority, bearing in mind the need to ensure homogeneous rules across the
EEA, considers that the Proposal, to the extent it is aligned on the FCR methodology
set for the EU by ACER Decision No. 12/2021, complies with the above requirements.
(36) Furthermore, the Authority, in cooperation with ACER, assessed compliance of the
Proposal with the requirements incorporated into the EEA Agreement under the FCA
Regulation and as recalled above, and with due regard to the amendments made by
ACER to the equivalent proposal for the EU in its Decision No. 07/2023. The Authority
raises the following considerations in support of its amendment to the Proposal.
6.2.1.Legal assessment of the requirements in Article 61 of the FCA Regulation (Cost of
ensuring firmness and remuneration of long-term transmission rights)
(37) Article 61(1) of the FCA Regulation requires that the Proposal covers the cost of
ensuring firmness which includes costs incurred from compensation mechanisms
associated with ensuring firmness of cross-zonal capacities as well as the cost of re-
dispatching, countertrading and imbalance associated with compensating market
participants.

Page 7
(38) In its Articles 3, 4 and 5, the Proposal provides for the costs of remuneration of eligible
LTTRs attributed to the implicit daily allocation process based on flow-based capacity
calculation and coordinated net transmission capacity (‘cNTC’) calculation, to be
shared among and paid by the TSOs.
(39) Article 6 of the Proposal requires that the costs for ensuring firmness of LTTRs are
shared and paid by the TSOs. Article 1(6) of the Proposal further clarifies that
imbalance costs do not occur in case of curtailed LTTRs.
(40) Article 1(5) of the Proposal includes provisions on re-dispatching and countertrading
by referring to ‘costly remedial actions’ and the provisions set out in Article 74(1) of
the CACM Regulation.11
(41) Given the above, the Proposal complies with Article 61(1) of the FCA Regulation.
(42) Article 61(3) of the FCA Regulation requires that the Proposal is consistent with the
FCA-CID methodology,12 whereby the latter methodology needs to take into account
the CACM-CID methodology13 (as required by Article 57(2) of the FCA Regulation).
(43) Articles 3 and 4 of the Proposal provide for an approach as to how to consider the
costs of remuneration of eligible LTTRs attributed to the implicit daily allocation
process based on flow-based capacity calculation and cNTC calculation. The
proposed approach is to share costs per market time unit, as used in the FCA-CID
methodology and the CACM-CID methodology. Further, following the principles
described in Articles 3 and 4 of the Proposal, distribution of these costs should follow
the same proportional distributions as the distribution of congestion income in
accordance with the FCA-CID methodology and the CACM-CID methodology.
(44) For clarification and consistency with the CACM-CID methodology, the Authority has
replaced the reference to ‘external borders’ in Article 4(6) of the Proposal with ‘slack
hubs’ and added the related definition of ‘slack hubs’ in Article 2(1) of the Proposal.
The Authority has also added clarifications to Article 3, paragraphs (4) and (5), of the
Proposal, to ensure consistency with the corresponding provisions in paragraphs (3)
and (4) of Article 4 of the Proposal, which were proposed by the TSOs.
(45) Furthermore, consistency with the related CID methodologies is ensured by Article 7
of the Proposal which, in paragraphs (3) and (4), requires an amendment of the FRC
methodology in case of amendments to the CID methodologies.
(46) Therefore, the Proposal complies with Article 61(3) of the FCA Regulation.
6.2.2.Assessment of the requirements of Article 4(8) of the FCA Regulation
(47) The Proposal meets the requirement of Article 4(8) of the FCA Regulation concerning
the implementation timescale, because it contains, in Article 7(2) of the Proposal, a
timeline for its implementation.
11 OJ L 197, 25.7.2015, p. 24, as incorporated into the EEA Agreement by EEA Joint Committee
Decision No. 204/2020 of 11 December 2020.
12 EFTA Surveillance Authority Decision No. 201/2025/COL of 3 December 2025 on the forward
capacity allocation congestion income distribution methodology for Norway. The Decision can be
found under this link:
https://www.eftasurv.int/cms/sites/default/files/documents/gopro/Case%2089429%20College%20D
ecision%20201%2025%20COL%20-%20Norway%20-
%20Decision%20on%20the%20congestion%20income%20distribution%20%28CID%29%20meth
odology.pdf.
13 EFTA Surveillance Authority Decision No. 074/25/COL of 28 May 2025 on the congestion income
distribution methodology for Norway. The Decision can be found under this link:
https://www.eftasurv.int/cms/sites/default/files/documents/gopro/Case%2091961%20College%20D
ecision%20074%2025%20COL%20-%20Norway%20-
%20Decision%20on%20the%20congestion%20income%20distribution%20methodology.pdf.

Page 8
(48) Article 4(8) of the FCA Regulation also requires the Proposal for amendment to
include a description of its impact on the objectives of the FCA Regulation. The
Proposal complies with this requirement by assessing the Proposal against the
objectives of the FCA Regulation in recitals (11) to (16) of the ‘Whereas’-section. The
Authority has introduced a number of amendments to these recitals to clarify and
complete the assessment.
6.2.3.Assessment of other points of the Proposal
(49) The Authority found it necessary to add a new paragraph (2) in Article 1 of the
Proposal, which describes the scope of application of the FRC methodology. The
new recital (5) explains the categories of TSOs for which the non-application of the
FRC methodology is legally justified.
(50) In that regard, the Authority took into account the exchange reflected in ACER
Decision No. 07/2023 for the EU.
(51) The Authority clarified the consideration of costs from the returns of LTTRs in Article
4(3) of Annex I to this Decision, and, as a consequence, deleted the reference to
returned LTTRs in Article 4(2) of the Proposal. The Authority also added a
corresponding provision in Article 3(5) of the Proposal.
(52) The Authority further introduced some necessary editorial changes to improve the
readability and the structure of the Proposal.
(53) The Authority made changes in the ‘Whereas’-section, Article 1(2) and Article 2 of
the Proposal to reflect the application of this Decision for Norway, the applicable legal
framework and the adaptations foreseen for the incorporation of the relevant legal
framework into the EEA Agreement. The Authority also amended Article 7 of the
Proposal to reflect the procedure set out in point 47 of Annex IV to the EEA
Agreement for the determination of the FRC methodology for Norway.14
7 CONCLUSION
(54) For the above reasons, the Authority considers that the amendments detailed in
section 6 of this Decision are necessary to ensure that the Proposal is in line with the
requirements and the objectives of the FCA Regulation, as well as to improve the
editorial quality.
(55) Therefore, the Authority approves the Proposal subject to the necessary substantive
and editorial amendments. Annex I to this Decision sets out the FRC methodology
for Norway, as amended and approved by the Authority.
14 For the avoidance of doubt, deadlines set out in the Proposal, as amended by the Authority, that
precede the date of implementation of the FRC methodology annexed to the present Decision are
to be understood as references to the date of implementation of this methodology.

Page 9
HAS ADOPTED THIS DECISION:
1. This Decision applies to Norway.
2. The determination of the FRC methodology for Norway, pursuant to Article 61 of
the FCA Regulation, is approved as set out in Annex I to this Decision. Annex I
forms an integral part of this Decision.
3. This Decision is addressed to RME.
4. This Decision enters into force upon notification to RME.
5. This Decision is authentic in the English language.
Done at Brussels,
For the EFTA Surveillance Authority
For Arne Røksund Árni Páll Árnason Nuscha Wieczorek
President Responsible College Member College Member
For Melpo-Menie Joséphidès
Countersigning as Director,
Legal and Executive Affairs
This document has been electronically authenticated by Arni Pall Arnason, Sigrun
Ingibjorg Gisladottir.

title: Case 89428: College Decision 059/26/COL - Norway - Requirements for the Single Allocation Platform (SAP)
text:
Case No: 89428
Document No: 1577795
Decision No: 059/26/COL
EFTA SURVEILLANCE AUTHORITY DECISION
of 18 March 2026
on the proposal for the requirements for the Single Allocation Platform (SAP) and
the SAP cost sharing methodology for Norway
THE EFTA SURVEILLANCE AUTHORITY,
Having regard to:
the Agreement on the European Economic Area1 (‘the EEA Agreement’), in particular to
Article 24 and Annex IV thereof,
the Act referred to at point 47 of Annex IV to the EEA Agreement:
Regulation (EC) No 713/2009 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 13
July 2009 establishing an Agency for the Cooperation of Energy Regulators (‘the
ACER Regulation’),2 and in particular Article 8 thereof, as adapted to the EEA
Agreement,3
the procedure referred to at point 47(d) of Annex IV to the EEA Agreement,
the Act referred to at point 50 of Annex IV to the EEA Agreement:
Commission Regulation (EU) 2016/1719 of 26 September 2016 establishing a
guideline on forward capacity allocation (‘FCA Regulation),4 and in particular
Articles 4(6)(c), 4(6)(f), 49 and 59 thereof, as adapted to the EEA Agreement,5
Whereas:
1 OJ L 1, 3.1.1994, p. 3.
2 OJ L 211, 14.8.2009, p. 1.
3 EEA Joint Committee Decision No. 93/2017 of 5 May 2017.
4 OJ L 259, 27.9.2016, p. 42.
5 EEA Joint Committee Decision No. 205/2020 of 11 December 2020.
Avenue des Arts 19H, 1000 Brussels, tel: +32 2 286 18 11, www.eftasurv.int

Page 2
1 INTRODUCTION
(1) The FCA Regulation lays down rules on cross-zonal capacity allocation in the forward
markets. These rules include requirements for the single allocation platform (‘SAP’)
and the SAP cost sharing methodology, hereafter collectively referred to as the ‘SAP
methodology’.
(2) Norway is part of the internal market for electricity through the EEA Agreement. The
objective of the EEA Agreement is to create a homogeneous European Economic
Area (‘EEA’). Relevant EU legislation for the internal electricity market is incorporated
into the EEA Agreement so that it applies throughout the whole EEA, ensuring
uniform application of laws relating to this market and thus ensuring a level playing
field throughout the EEA. Upon incorporation of the EU legislation into the EEA
Agreement, Norway has the obligation to implement the acquis covered by this
legislation.
(3) The FCA Regulation was incorporated into the EEA Agreement by Decision of the
EEA Joint Committee No. 205/2020, which entered into force on 31 July 2021.6 It is
therefore appropriate to apply the FCA Regulation’s requirements for the SAP to
Norway.
(4) The present Decision follows from the request of the Norwegian regulatory authority
(‘RME’) that the EFTA Surveillance Authority (‘the Authority’) adopts a decision for
Norway on the proposal it received from the Norwegian transmission system operator
(‘TSO’), Statnett, for (i) the establishment of an SAP in accordance with Article 49 of
the FCA Regulation and (ii) the SAP cost sharing methodology in accordance with
Article 59 of the FCA Regulation, respectively (‘the Proposal’).
(5) The present Decision includes the following annex:
Annex I to Decision No. 059/26/COL of 18 March 2026 on the proposal for
the requirements for the Single Allocation Platform (SAP) and the
SAP cost sharing methodology for Norway, determining the
requirements for the Single Allocation Platform (SAP) and the SAP
cost sharing methodology in accordance with Articles 49 and 59 of
Commission Regulation (EU) 2016/1719 establishing a guideline on
forward capacity allocation for Norway, as amended and approved
by the EFTA Surveillance Authority (Document No. 1577796).
For information purposes only, the Authority’s Decision is accompanied by a text
providing a track-changed version of the Proposal, reflecting the Authority’s
amendments (Document No. 1589460).
2 PROCEDURE
(6) On 20 October 2022, the European Network of Transmission System Operators for
Electricity (‘ENTSO-E’) submitted the Proposal to RME on behalf of the Norwegian
TSO.
(7) RME requested on 3 March 2023 (Documents No. 1357183, 1357181, 1357184 and
1357182) the Authority to decide on the Proposal in accordance with point 47 of
Annex IV to the EEA Agreement, in particular the adaptations set out in paragraph
(d) thereof for the application of Article 8(1)(b) of the ACER Regulation, as
incorporated into the EEA Agreement by Decision of the EEA Joint Committee No.
93/2017, and Article 4(10) of the FCA Regulation, as incorporated into the EEA
6 The FCA Regulation was made binding in the internal legal order in Norway through national
measures, which entered into force on 1 August 2021.

Page 3
Agreement by Decision of the EEA Joint Committee No. 205/2020 and having regard
in particular to paragraph (9) of the preamble thereof.
(8) On 3 July 2023, the Authority requested the European Union Agency for the
Cooperation of Energy Regulators (‘ACER’) to prepare a draft for the Authority’s
decision on the Proposal in accordance with point 47(d)(iv) of Annex IV to the EEA
Agreement (Document No. 1384919).
(9) On 12 December 2023, ACER provided the Authority with a draft for the Authority’s
decision on the Proposal, in accordance with point 47(d)(iv) of Annex IV to the EEA
Agreement.7
(10) On 10 February 2026, the Authority invited RME to express its views on the
Authority’s preliminary decision, in accordance with point 47(d)(iv) of Annex IV to the
EEA Agreement (Documents No. 1589734, 1589737, 1589736 and 1589735).
(11) On 26 February 2026, RME shared its views on the Authority’s preliminary decision
(Document No. 1592905). These views are summarised in section 5 of this Decision.
(12) This Decision is issued following the Authority’s review and amendment of the
Proposal, in cooperation with ACER. Annex I to this Decision sets for Norway the
SAP methodology, pursuant to Articles 49 and 59 of the FCA Regulation, as
amended and approved by the Authority.
(13) The Authority’s Decision determines the SAP methodology solely for Norway.
(14) For the purposes of this Decision, it is relevant to note that the SAP methodology for
the EU has been subject of two ACER Decisions, namely Decisions No. 09/2022
(‘ACER Decision No. 09/2022’)8 and 05/2023 (‘ACER Decision No. 05/2023’),9 both
amending the initial SAP methodology set by national regulatory authorities for the
EU. The Proposal, received by the Authority from RME, corresponds to the
consolidated SAP methodology for the EU set by ACER Decision No. 09/2022 and
including proposed amendments upon which ACER decided for the EU by Decision
No. 05/2023 (‘EU Proposal’).
(15) ACER, when taking its Decisions No. 09/2022 and 05/2023, held numerous dialogues
and exchanges with the relevant EEA TSOs and national regulatory authorities. The
dialogues and exchanges are documented in detail in ACER Decisions No. 09/2022
and 05/2023 for the EU.
3 THE AUTHORITY’S COMPETENCE TO DECIDE ON THE PROPOSAL
(16) In accordance with point 47(d)(i) of Annex IV to the EEA Agreement, adapting Article
8 of the ACER Regulation, in cases involving one or more EFTA State(s), the
7 Documents No. 1424514, 1424515, 1424516, 1424517, 1424518 and 1424519.
8 ACER Decision No. 09/2022 of 18 July 2022 on the TSOs‘ proposal for amendment to the
requirements of the Single Allocation Platform and to the methodology for sharing costs of
establishing, developing and operating the Single Allocation Platform. The decision is available
under this link:
https://acer.europa.eu/sites/default/files/documents/Individual%20Decisions/ACER%20Decision%
2009-2022%20on%20amendments%20to%20SAP.pdf. Annex I to ACER Decision No. 09/2022 is
available under this link:
https://acer.europa.eu/sites/default/files/documents/Individual%20Decisions_annex/ACER%20Dec
ision%2009-2022%20on%20amendments%20to%20SAP%20-%20Annex%20I.pdf. The decision
was limited to the scope of application of the methodology.
9 ACER Decision No. 05/2023 of 22 March 2023 on the TSOs’ proposal for amendments to the
requirements for the Single Allocation Platform (SAP) and the SAP cost sharing methodology. The
decision is available under this link:
https://acer.europa.eu/sites/default/files/documents/Individual%20Decisions/ACER_Decision_05-
2023_on_SAP.pdf. Annex I to ACER Decision No. 05/2023 is available under this link:
https://acer.europa.eu/sites/default/files/documents/Individual%20Decisions_annex/ACER_Decisio
n_05-2023_on_SAP_Annex_I.pdf.

Page 4
Authority is to adopt a decision addressed to the national regulatory authorities of the
concerned EFTA State(s), where ACER is competent on the EU side.
(17) On 3 March 2023, RME referred the Proposal to the Authority for a decision in
accordance with point 47(d)(i) of Annex IV to the EEA Agreement, adapting Article
8(1)(b) of the ACER Regulation.
(18) The Proposal’s content falls within the scope of Articles 49 and 59 of the FCA
Regulation. The FCA Regulation was incorporated at point 50 of Annex IV to the EEA
Agreement by Decision of the EEA Joint Committee No. 205/2020, which entered
into force on 31 July 2021.
(19) Following the incorporation of the FCA Regulation into the EEA Agreement, the
Proposal involves one EFTA State, namely Norway. In the EU, ACER decided on the
corresponding proposal through binding decisions.
(20) Consequently, it is the Authority’s competence to adopt a decision addressed to RME
to determine the SAP methodology for Norway.
4 SUMMARY OF THE PROPOSAL
(21) The Proposal includes the following elements:
(a) ‘Whereas’ section;
(b) General provisions, including subject matter and scope of application,
definitions, and implementation in Part 1;
(c) Part 2 with governance rules and functional requirements;
i. general requirements on the scope and SAP council;
ii. provisions on SAP cooperation agreement (‘SAP CA’);
iii. harmonised contractual framework with market participants;
iv. principles of financial settlement and risk management;
v. products, allocation methods and algorithms;
vi. operational processes;
vii. data interfaces; and
viii. technical availability and reliability of provided tasks;
(d) Part 3 with SAP cost sharing;
(e) Annex, with requirements for the long-term flow-based allocation (‘LTFBA’)
algorithm.
5 OBSERVATIONS RECEIVED BY THE AUTHORITY
(22) RME observed that the Authority’s preliminary decision supported the functional
requirements for running the SAP, including provisions for governance, cooperation
and cost-sharing in accordance with Articles 49 and 59 of the FCA Regulation.
(23) RME further considered that the methodology as annexed to the Authority’s
preliminary decision supported the objectives in Article 3 of the FCA Regulation by
harmonising and simplifying long-term cross-zonal trading through a single platform.
RME noted that the methodology centralised and optimised capacity allocation with
transparent rules and a unified contractual framework, ensuring fair, efficient and
non-discriminatory access for all market participants. RME further stressed that the
methodology also guaranteed equal treatment through common algorithms, provided
a single reliable source of information, and supported orderly price formation. Overall,

Page 5
RME considered that the methodology enhanced effective long-term operation and
development of the electricity system across the EEA.
(24) RME especially welcomed the adaptations made by the Authority to reflect the
relevant legal framework of the FCA Regulation and the Third Energy Package, as
incorporated into the EEA Agreement. RME considered that these adaptations were
not only necessary, but appropriate and well-balanced against the need to ensure a
homogeneous approach, such approach being essential to ensure the functioning of
the internal market.
(25) RME found that the content of the Authority’s preliminary decision fell within the
scope of Articles 49 and 59 of the FCA Regulation. It also found that the FCA
Regulation and Regulation (EC) No 714/2009 of the European Parliament and of the
Council of 13 July 2009 on conditions for access to the network for cross-border
exchanges in electricity and repealing Regulation (EC) No 1228/2003 (‘the Electricity
Regulation’)10 provided the relevant framework for the determination of the SAP
methodology for Norway, as set out in the Authority’s preliminary decision.
6 ASSESSMENT OF THE PROPOSAL
6.1. Legal framework
(26) Articles 4(6)(c) and 4(6)(f) of the FCA Regulation provide that the requirements for
the SAP and the methodology for sharing costs of establishing, developing and
operating the SAP, in accordance with Articles 49 and 59 of the same Regulation,
are subject to approval.
(27) Article 4(8) of the FCA Regulation requires the Proposal to include a proposed
timescale for its implementation and a description of the expected impact on the
objectives of the FCA Regulation.
(28) Article 49 of the FCA Regulation sets out specific requirements for the SAP and refers
to Article 50 for the general tasks that have to be covered by the SAP, as well as to
Article 59 for sharing costs of establishing, developing and operating the SAP. These
articles do not require a consultation of stakeholders on the draft proposals
concerning those terms, conditions and methodology.
(29) Article 30(7) of the FCA Regulation provides that Articles 49 and 59 of the same
Regulation shall not apply to the TSOs of the bidding zone borders in the event that
regulatory authorities decide that long-term transmission rights (‘LTTRs’) shall not be
issued by the respective TSOs or that other long-term cross-zonal hedging products
shall be made available by the respective TSOs.
(30) As outlined in section 1 of this Decision, Article 1 of the EEA Agreement defines the
aim of the EEA Agreement as achieving a homogeneous EEA based on common
rules and equal conditions of competition.
(31) The Decisions of the EEA Joint Committee No. 93/2017 and No. 205/2020 set up a
mechanism catering for homogeneous interpretation and application of the ACER
Regulation and the FCA Regulation throughout the EEA. Under these acts, the
Authority is the competent authority to issue binding decisions vis-à-vis the
concerned national regulatory authorities of the EFTA States in cases involving one
or more EFTA State(s), where ACER is the competent authority on the EU side. The
decisions of the Authority are to be based on drafts prepared by ACER. The Authority
is to invite the national regulatory authorities of the EFTA States to express their
10 OJ L 211, 14.8.2009, p. 15, as incorporated at point 20 of Annex IV to the EEA Agreement by
Decision of the EEA Joint Committee No. 93/2017 of 5 May 2017.

Page 6
views on the matter, taking full account of the urgency, complexity and potential
consequences of the matter.
6.2. Assessment of the legal requirements
(32) The Proposal provides the SAP methodology for Norway.
(33) Considering the objective of the EEA Agreement to achieve a homogeneous EEA
based on common rules, as emphasised in sections 1 and 6.1 of this Decision, the
Authority is to adopt decisions consistent with decisions taken by ACER on similar
matters.
(34) The essence of the FCA Regulation and SAP methodology especially calls for a
homogeneous approach. It requires the establishment of an SAP, for the allocation
of LTTRs through harmonised rules and operated by a joint allocation office (‘JAO’).
The SAP has in practice already been established. As a consequence, any divergent
requirement for the SAP methodology for Norway would alter the SAP and thus the
good functioning of the internal electricity market.
(35) The Authority, bearing in mind the need to ensure homogeneity across the EEA,
considers that the Proposal, to the extent it is aligned on the SAP methodology set
for the EU by ACER Decision No. 09/2022, complies with the above requirements.
(36) Furthermore, the Authority, in cooperation with ACER, assessed the compliance of
the Proposal with the requirements incorporated into the EEA Agreement under the
FCA Regulation and here above recalled, and with due consideration to the
amendments made by ACER to the equivalent proposal for the EU in its Decision No.
05/2023. It raises the following considerations in support of its amendment to the
Proposal.
6.2.1.Legal assessment of the requirements in Article 4(8) of the FCA Regulation (timeline
and impact)
(37) Article 4(8) of the FCA Regulation requires the Proposal to include a proposed
timescale for its implementation. This provision is included in Article 4 of the Proposal
(Article 3 in Annex I, after the Authority’s amendments).
(a) The Proposal sets the timeline for SAP implementation to 12 (twelve) months
after the approval of the SAP methodology, except for direct current (‘DC’)
interconnectors for which it was set to 24 (twenty-four) months after the
approval of the SAP methodology.
(b) The Authority considered the requirements of the capacity calculation
methodologies of the Core and Nordic11 capacity calculation regions (‘CCRs’)
and the timeline for application of the long-term flow-based capacity allocation
defined in those decisions. The Authority also analysed the specific
requirements of the application of the evolved flow-based (‘EFB’) approach on
the bidding zone borders internal and external to the flow-based CCRs.
Aligning on ACER’s approach for its Decision No. 05/2023 for the EU and
reflecting the TSOs’ input with regard to the deadline, the Authority set the
following phases and deadlines:
i. The first phase should be completed by the time of the yearly auction for
2025; it should enable the flow-based allocation and include the
11 As approved by the Authority for Norway by EFTA Surveillance Authority Decision No.
038/25/COL of 2 April 2025 on the Nordic CCR long-term capacity calculation methodology for
Norway. The Decision can be found under this link:
https://www.eftasurv.int/cms/sites/default/files/documents/gopro/Case 93078 College Decision 038
25 COL - Norway - Decision on the long-term capacity calculation methodology.pdf.

Page 7
application of the EFB approach at high voltage direct current (‘HVDC’)
interconnectors internal to a flow-based CCR.
ii. The second phase should be completed by the time of the yearly auction
for 2026; it should enable the application of the EFB approach at HVDC
interconnectors external to a flow-based CCR. This involves the
application of EFB at bidding zone borders between the CCRs applying
flow-based allocation and the CCRs applying coordinated net
transmission capacity (‘cNTC’) allocation (for HVDC interconnectors, and
special cases of radial and non-meshed alternative current (‘AC’)
borders).
iii. The third phase should be completed by the time of the yearly auction for
2027; it should enable the application of single integrated flow-based
allocation for the Core and Nordic CCRs, with the EFB approach
between these CCRs.
iv. The Authority also added a paragraph clarifying that any other
requirements (such as the allocation of LTTRs using the cNTC approach)
should be applicable by the time of approval of the SAP methodology,
since these should either be implemented already or can be directly
applied.
(38) Article 4(8) of the FCA Regulation also requires the Proposal to include a description
of its impact on the objectives of the FCA Regulation. The Authority considered that
the ‘Whereas’-section of the Proposal did not clearly enough assess the impact on
the objectives of the FCA Regulation. It therefore included recitals (15) to (23) in the
‘Whereas’-section of Annex I of this Decision to ensure a consistent assessment of
the SAP methodology against the objectives of the FCA Regulation.
6.2.2.Assessment of the requirements in Articles 49 and 50 of the FCA Regulation
(functional requirements and general tasks)
(39) Article 49(2) of the FCA Regulation lists the minimal set of functional requirements of
the SAP platform. In the following recitals, the Authority checked the Proposal against
these requirements, taking into account ACER Decision No. 05/2023 for the EU, and
explained potential necessary changes made by the Authority.12
(40) Article 49(2)(a) of the FCA Regulation requires the inclusion of the expected bidding
zone borders to be covered.
(a) The ‘Whereas’-section of the Proposal included the list of TSOs responsible for
the development of the proposal, and Article 1 generally described the
applicability of the SAP methodology, however without listing TSOs or bidding
zone borders;
(b) The Authority considered that the list of TSOs responsible for the development
of the Proposal is not relevant for the approved SAP methodology and therefore
deleted it;
(c) In order to fulfil the requirements of Article 49(2)(a), the Authority amended the
‘Whereas’-section (recital (5)) and Article 1. In paragraph (2) of Article 1, the
Authority clarified that the expected bidding zone borders to be covered by the
SAP are specified through the harmonised allocation rules methodology
(‘HAR’).13
12 Unless otherwise specified, the numbering of titles and articles is in accordance with the Proposal
as amended by the Authority.
13 As approved by the Authority for Norway by EFTA Surveillance Authority Decision of 8 October
2025 on the harmonised allocation rules for long-term transmission rights for Norway. The Decision
can be found under this link: https://www.eftasurv.int/cms/sites/default/files/documents/gopro/Case

Page 8
(41) Article 49(2)(b) of the FCA Regulation requires the inclusion of the technical
availability and reliability of provided services.
(a) The Proposal provided these provisions in its Articles 56 to 59 (title 9).
(b) Article 59 (‘Management of participants’ claims’) did not provide full clarity of
the treatment of participants’ claims. Therefore, the Authority included a clear
sequence for the required steps to be taken by the SAP operator.
(42) Article 49(2)(c) of the FCA Regulation requires the inclusion of the operational
processes.
(a) The Proposal includes these provisions in its Articles 42 to 53 (title 7).
(b) Apart from small wording changes, the Authority did not make substantial
changes to these articles.
(43) Article 49(2)(d) of the FCA Regulation requires the inclusion of offered products.
(a) The Proposal provided these provisions in its Article 38(2).
(b) Aligning on ACER Decision No. 05/2023 for the EU and the outcomes of
ACER’s interactions with the TSOs, the Authority reworded paragraph (2) of
this article, in order to establish a relation to the HAR.
(44) Article 49(2)(e) of the FCA Regulation requires the inclusion of forward capacity
allocation time frames.
(a) The Proposal provided these provisions in its Article 38(1), specifying at least
yearly and monthly timeframes, unless otherwise specified by the HAR
methodology.
(b) The Authority did not change this provision.
(45) Article 49(2)(g) of the FCA Regulation requires the inclusion of principles of financial
settlement and risk management of allocated products.
(a) The Proposal provided these provisions in its Articles 32 to 36 (title 5).
(b) The Authority acknowledges the TSOs’ request in the framework of ACER
Decision No. 05/2023 for the EU to have flexibility with regard to collaterals and
credit limit in the SAP methodology as they would be specified in the HAR
methodology. To consider such proposed amendment, the TSOs suggested
specific changes to the text of Article 33 of the Proposal.
(c) Aligning on ACER Decision No. 05/2023 for the EU, the Authority agrees to
establish sufficient flexibility in the SAP methodology and allow the HAR
methodology to further specify appropriate collateral requirements. Therefore,
to offer such flexibility, the Authority amended Article 32(2) and Article 33(1) of
the Proposal by deleting the restriction to specifying forms of allowed
collaterals. The Authority did not make further amendments, considering that
these provisions leave sufficient flexibility for the HAR methodology, while
ensuring that the SAP operator is in a position to fulfil these requirements
regarding the processes related to collaterals if needed.
(46) Article 49(2)(h) of the FCA Regulation requires the inclusion of a harmonised
contractual framework with market participants.
(a) The Proposal provided these provisions in its Article 31 (title 4).
(b) Apart from small wording changes, the Authority did not make substantial
changes to these articles.
(47) Article 49(2)(i) of the FCA Regulation requires the inclusion of the data interfaces.
93079 College Decision 167 25 COL - Norway - Decision on the harmonised allocation rules for
long-term transmi.pdf.

Page 9
(a) The Proposal provided these provisions in its Articles 54 to 55 (title 8).
(b) Apart from small wording changes, the Authority did not make substantial
changes to these articles.
(48) Article 49(1) of the FCA Regulation requires that the proposal for the set of
requirements and for the establishment of the SAP identifies different options for the
establishment and governance of the SAP. Aligning on ACER Decision No. 05/2023
for the EU and considering that the SAP has already been established, the following
legal assessment focuses only on the governance provisions.
(a) The Proposal provided these provisions in its Articles 5 to 6 (title 2), as well as
its Articles 8 to 30 (title 3 on SAP cooperation agreement).
(b) The Proposal included the designation of the entity operating the SAP, namely
JAO, as well as the reasons for this designation in the ‘Whereas’-section. To
ensure sufficient clarity, the Authority specified, aligning on ACER’s agreement
with all TSOs and reflected in its Decision No. 05/2023 for the EU, this
designation of JAO in Article 5 of the Proposal.
(c) The Authority also included in Article 5(2) of the Proposal provisions which
ensure the TSOs’ accountability and the possibility of regulatory oversight also
in a case of delegation of tasks by the SAP operator to third parties.
(d) Article 6 of the Proposal includes provisions related to the SAP council. In
paragraph (6) of this article, the Authority added the SAP task of deciding on
the reporting and publication of information, as provided in Article 7.
(e) The Authority added Article 7 to the Proposal (‘Transparency, publication,
monitoring and reporting’) to summarise all monitoring, publication and
reporting requirements under one article and ensure sufficient transparency,
aligning on ACER’s approach in its Decision No. 05/2023 for the EU following
input from the TSOs with regard to reporting, the publication of possibly
sensitive and/or confidential information and information requests.
(f) In Articles 8 to 30 of the Proposal, related to the SAP cooperation agreement
(‘SAP CA’), the Authority provided further small wording amendments,
clarifications and corrections. Besides the changes for improving the wording
and structure and adding relevant references to the provisions in these articles,
the Authority introduced the following further amendments for clarifications:
i. In Article 8 of the Proposal, the Authority added a provision ensuring that
arrangements allowed outside the scope of the SAP CA are consistent
with the SAP methodology.
ii. The Authority aligned the provision of Article 9(k) of the Proposal on
ACER’s approach in its Decision No. 05/2023 for the EU following
feedback from the TSOs.
iii. The Authority complemented Article 13 of the Proposal with the reference
to publication and publication requirements.
iv. In Article 14 of the Proposal, the Authority added a new paragraph to be
more explicit about the SAP operator’s obligation to allow for auditing by
TSOs.
v. In Article 15(2) of the Proposal, the Authority further specified what
should be considered as ‘agreed level of performance’.
vi. The Authority clarified in Articles 16 and 28 of the Proposal which entities
should be considered where the articles previously referred to ‘Party’
without further specifying the term ‘Party’.
vii. In Article 28 of the Proposal, the Authority specified the process of
termination of the SAP CA. More specifically, the Authority clarified:

Page 10
1. in paragraph (1), that the default option of termination of the SAP
CA is limited to TSOs which are no longer required to issue
LTTRs (e.g. following a new derogation in accordance with Article
30(7) of the FCA Regulation);
2. in paragraph (2), that the SAP operator is not part of the decision
process because it cannot be considered in such process which
is subject to qualified majority principles (decisions based on
qualified majority principles in accordance with Article 4(2) of the
FCA Regulation may not include any other parties than TSOs);
and
3. in paragraph (5), the SAP operator’s right to suspend the
provision of SAP tasks. The Authority deleted parts of this
paragraph since potential changes of increasing risk or liabilities
should in general not lead to a discontinuation of operations of
the SAP which could result in serious negative impacts on the
functioning of the European forward electricity markets. Further,
while the Authority does agree that the SAP operator should not
be required to do the impossible, the Authority does not deem it
necessary to address provisions of the HAR methodology which
are not possible to fulfil, since such provisions are subject to an
amendment process of the HAR methodology and should not be
proposed or approved if considered impossible.
viii. In Article 29 of the Proposal, the Authority revised provisions on force
majeure, since force majeure is already defined under Article 2(45) of
Commission Regulation (EU) 2015/1222 of 24 July 2015 establishing a
guideline on capacity allocation and congestion management (‘the
CACM Regulation’)14 and therefore also applicable to the SAP
methodology in accordance with Article 2 of the Proposal.
6.2.2.1. Assessment of the allocation methods and algorithms
(49) Article 49(2)(f) of the FCA Regulation requires the inclusion of the allocation methods
and algorithms.
(50) Articles 37 and 39 of the Proposal and the Proposal’s Annex refer to the application
of the LTFBA (besides the cNTC allocation approach) and the algorithms and
mathematical formulations for both the cNTC-based and the flow-based approach.
The Proposal also envisaged the application of the EFB approach, referring thereto
in a footnote in Article 39.
(51) The Authority considered that it is necessary to improve the structure of those
provisions by removing the Annex and including its provisions in other articles.
Therefore, the Authority reorganised those provisions in a way that:
(a) Article 39 ‘General requirements for long-term allocation algorithms’ covers
common provisions for both the cNTC and the flow-based approach, pointing
to their differences where necessary;
(b) Article 40 ‘Long-term flow-based allocation algorithm’ sets out the provisions
solely related to the flow-based approach;
(c) In order to align the mathematical formulation of the cNTC and flow-based
allocation approaches, the Authority introduced a new Article 41, providing this
mathematical formulation for both approaches in parallel columns of a table.
14 OJ L 197, 25.7.2015, p. 24, as incorporated into the EEA Agreement by EEA Joint Committee
Decision No. 204/2020 of 11 December 2020.

Page 11
(52) The Authority largely agreed with the Proposal’s provisions in relation to long-term
flow-based capacity allocation. However, the Authority considered it necessary to
provide further clarity and distinction of the particular provisions for the cNTC-based
and flow-based approaches, and applied them in Articles 39 to 41, and consequently
in Article 2 (‘Definitions’).
(53) In Article 2, the Authority removed the definition of ‘allocation constraint’, initially
proposed, to deal with border-wise constraints for the cNTC approach. The term
‘allocation constraint’ as defined in Article 2(6) of the CACM Regulation has a wider
meaning, not only related to the composite capacity constraint on a group of bidding
zone borders. Moreover, such a composite constraint is applicable not only for the
cNTC-based approach but for the flow-based approach as well. Therefore, the
Authority defined such a constraint in the SAP methodology, as ’external constraint’
(‘EC’), applicable for both the cNTC approach and the flow-based approach. Through
a footnote in Article 40, the Authority clarified that, if applied for all borders of a bidding
zone, such an EC becomes export/import limit, as a special case of EC.
(54) Aligning on ACER Decision No. 05/2023 for the EU, the Authority also provided in
Article 2 a definition of the EFB approach, as an approach to consider HVDC
interconnectors (as well as special cases of radial non-meshed AC bidding zone
borders) in flow-based capacity calculation and allocation, at bidding zone borders
internal or external to a flow-based CCR. The Authority is aware that these allocation
principles are similar to the advanced hybrid coupling (‘AHC’) approach as defined in
the Core Day Ahead Capacity Calculation methodology (for the application at the
bidding zone borders external to Core CCR), and that the same methodology uses
the term EFB for the consideration of only internal HVDC interconnectors in the Core
CCR. The Authority considers that using the term ‘AHC’ is not suitable for the long-
term allocation, as it refers to (price) coupling, which is applied only for day-ahead
and intraday time horizons. Therefore, the Authority found it appropriate to use the
term EFB for both internal and external bidding zone borders of a flow-based CCR.
(55) The Authority amended Article 39 of the Proposal by generalising it for both the cNTC
and the flow-based approach, or by adding specific notions for flow-based allocation,
such as the calculation of marginal clearing price and shadow price.
(56) In the same Article 39, the Authority confirmed the consideration of reduction periods.
The Authority considers reduction periods as applicable only for the bidding zone
borders applying the cNTC-based allocation approach. Furthermore, although
reduction periods may exist for the bidding zone borders applying the cNTC-based
approach, it is acceptable that the allocation algorithm does not consider them, since
they would not impact the result of an auction.
(57) In Articles 40 and 41, the Authority provided the provisions on:
(a) the application of the EFB approach;
(b) the application of external constraints for composite borders for both cNTC and
flow-based approaches;
(c) combined (grouped) critical network element and contingency (‘CNEC’)
constraints, as required by the Nordic long-term capacity calculation
methodology; and
(d) clearing price calculation for bidding zone-oriented borders.
(58) In paragraph (6) of Article 40, the Authority provided detailed requirements for the
publication of information with regard to flow-based allocation outputs.
(59) Aligning on ACER’s approach in its Decision No. 05/2023 for the EU following input
from the TSOs and national regulatory authorities, the Authority takes into account
that there is currently no application of financial transmission rights (‘FTR’)-
obligations, nor has it been announced by any TSO for any of the bidding zone
borders. Also, introducing FTR-obligations would require a revision of not only the

Page 12
SAP methodology but also other methodologies, such as the HAR methodology.
Therefore, the Authority included in Article 1 (‘Subject matter and scope’) a provision
which requires all TSOs responsible for developing this methodology to propose an
amendment to the SAP methodology before introducing FTR-obligations at any of
the bidding zone borders. The Authority also deleted paragraph (e) in Article 9 of the
Proposal, which referred to FTR obligations.
(60) Aligning on ACER’s approach in its Decision No. 05/2023 for the EU following input
from the TSOs, the Authority amended the definitions of flow-based allocation outputs
provided in Article 40(6) of the Proposal and extended the definitions of expected
outputs to provide additional clarity.
6.2.3.Assessment of the requirements in Article 59 of the FCA Regulation (SAP cost
sharing)
(61) Article 59 of the FCA Regulation requires that all TSOs issuing LTTRs on the SAP
jointly bear the costs related to its establishment and operation. The Proposal
provided these provisions in Articles 60 to 68 of Annex I to this Decision.
(62) However, the Proposal did not properly consider situations with multiple TSOs from
one side of a bidding zone border in Article 64 (‘Cost sharing arrangements’), and
therefore the Authority introduced provisions which treat such situations in line with
the TSOs’ sharing key for the long-term congestion income.
6.2.4.Assessment of other points of the Proposal (application to TSOs and editorial
changes)
(63) Aligning on ACER Decision No. 05/2023 for the EU, the Authority found it necessary
to clarify the scope of application of the SAP methodology by adding in the Proposal
a new paragraph (2) in Article 1 and a new recital (5) explaining the categories of
TSOs for which the non-application of the SAP methodology is legally justified.
(64) The Authority made changes in the ‘Whereas’ section and Article 1 of the Proposal
to reflect the application of this Decision for Norway, the applicable legal framework
and the adaptations foreseen for the incorporation of the relevant legal framework
into the EEA Agreement. The Authority also amended Article 3 of the Proposal to
reflect the procedure set out in point 47 of Annex IV to the EEA Agreement for the
determination of the SAP methodology for Norway.15
(65) Finally, the Authority introduced some necessary editorial changes to improve the
readability and the form of the SAP methodology. This includes removal of the
previous Annex 1 of the Proposal dealing with flow-based allocation principles, and
including the related provisions in other articles of the SAP methodology.
7 CONCLUSION
(66) For all the above reasons, the Authority considers the Proposal in line with the
requirements of the FCA Regulation, provided that the amendments described in this
Decision are integrated in the Proposal, as presented in Annex I to this Decision. The
amendments are necessary to ensure that the Proposal is in line with the purpose of
the FCA Regulation and contributes to market integration, non-discrimination,
effective competition and the proper functioning of the market.
15 For the avoidance of doubt, deadlines set out in the Proposal, as amended by the Authority, that
precede the date of approval of the SAP methodology annexed to the present Decision are to be
understood as references to the date of approval of this methodology.

Page 13
(67) Therefore, the Authority approves the Proposal subject to the necessary
amendments. Annex I to this Decision sets out the SAP methodology for Norway, as
amended and approved by the Authority.
HAS ADOPTED THIS DECISION:
1. This Decision applies to Norway.
2. The SAP methodology for Norway, pursuant to Articles 49 and 59 of the FCA
Regulation, is approved as set out in Annex I to this Decision. Annex I forms an
integral part of this Decision.
3. This Decision is addressed to RME.
4. This Decision enters into force upon notification to RME.
5. This Decision is authentic in the English language.
Done at Brussels,
For the EFTA Surveillance Authority
For Arne Røksund Árni Páll Árnason Nuscha Wieczorek
President Responsible College Member College Member
For Melpo-Menie Joséphidès
Countersigning as Director,
Legal and Executive Affairs
This document has been electronically authenticated by Arni Pall Arnason, Sigrun
Ingibjorg Gisladottir.

title: Case 93403: Annex to College Decision 052/26/COL - State aid - Norway Iceland Liechtenstein - 2025 Amendments to the Guidelines on certain State aid measures in the context of the system for greenhouse gas emission allowance trading post-2021
text:
Case No: 93403
Document No: 1587829
Annex to Decision No: 052/26/COL
Amendments to the Guidelines on certain State aid measures in the context of
the system for greenhouse gas emission allowance trading post-2021:
The Guidelines on certain State aid measures in the context of the system for greenhouse gas
emission allowance trading post-2021 (1) are amended as follows:
1. point (15) (10) is replaced by the following:
‘‘CO2 emission factor’, in tCO2/MWh, means the weighted average of the CO2
intensity of electricity produced from fossil fuels in different geographic areas.
The weight reflects the production mix of the fossil fuels in the given geographic
area. The CO2 factor is the result of the division of the CO2 equivalent
emission data of the energy industry divided by the gross electricity generation
based on fossil fuels in TWh. For the purposes of these Guidelines*, the areas
are defined as geographic zones (a) which consist of submarkets coupled
through power exchanges; or (b) within which no declared congestion exists
and, in both cases, hourly day-ahead power exchange prices within the zones
showing price divergence in euro (using daily ECB exchange rates) of
maximum 1 % in a significant number of all hours in a year. Such regional
differentiation reflects the significance of fossil fuel plants for the final price set
on the wholesale market and their role as marginal plants in the merit order.
The mere fact that electricity is traded between two EEA States does not
automatically mean that they constitute a supranational region. Given the lack
of relevant data at sub-national level, the geographic areas comprise the entire
territory of one or more EEA States. On this basis, the following geographic
areas can be identified: Spain and Portugal; Lithuania, Latvia and Estonia;
Germany and Luxembourg; Bulgaria and Romania; and all other EEA States
separately, including Iceland and Norway**. The corresponding maximum
regional CO2 factors, which apply as maximal values when the notifying EEA
EFTA State has not established an assessment of the market-based CO
2
factor pursuant to point (11), are listed in Annex III. An EEA EFTA State may
ask ESA to calculate the CO emission factor based on data available in 2026 and
2
may notify the resulting CO emission factor as an amendment to its scheme. This
2
updated CO emission factor can apply to costs incurred as from 2026. This
2
updated CO emission factor has no effect on the CO emission factor of other
2 2
EEA States as listed in Annex III. In order to ensure equal treatment of sources
of electricity and avoid possible abuses, the same CO2 emission factor applies
to all sources of electricity supply (auto generation, electricity supply contracts
or grid supply) and to all aid beneficiaries in the EEA State concerned. If the
maximum regional CO2 emission factor listed in Annex III is at least 15 %
lower than the maximum regional CO2 emission factor previously listed in that
Annex or the approved market-based factor before 1 January 2026, EEA EFTA
States concerned may notify a transitional period to move from the previously
applicable maximum regional CO2 emission factor to the updated maximum
regional CO2 emission factor under these Guidelines for year t, starting in
2026, in equal annual steps of reduction. The updated maximum regional CO2
emission factor listed in Annex III shall apply for year t = 2030 at the latest;’
* These Guidelines do not qualify as legislative instruments and therefore do
not have to be incorporated into the EEA Agreement by the EEA Joint
Committee. ESA is responsible for setting the relevant rules applicable for the
EEA EFTA States, including the methodology for setting the CO2 factors. See
(1) OJ L 130, 15.4.2021, p. 3, EEA Supplement No 27, 15.4.2021, p. 3, as supplemented by OJ L 204, 4.8.2022,
p. 3, EEA Supplement No 51, 4. 8.2022, p. 1.

further paragraph 69 of these Guidelines.
** The geographic area for Liechtenstein and the applicable CO emissions
2
factor will be established at a later stage.
2. point (21) is replaced by the following:
‘To limit the risk of competition distortion within the internal market, the aid must
be limited to sectors that are exposed to a genuine risk of carbon leakage
due to significant indirect costs that are actually incurred as a consequence of
greenhouse gas emission costs being passed on in electricity prices. For the
purpose of these Guidelines, a genuine risk of carbon leakage is considered to
exist if the beneficiary is active in a sector listed in Annex I. A sector or subsector
that is not listed in Annex I but meets the criteria to be included in that Annex *,
may also be considered eligible provided that EEA EFTA States demonstrate
this with data that is representative of the sector or subsector at EEA level,
verified by an independent expert and based on a time period of at least the
three most recent years for which data is available. If EEA EFTA States intend
to extend their respective schemes to include any new sector or subsector, they
must notify any such amendment to ESA. EEA EFTA States may provide in their
schemes a commitment to include in the future all additional sectors or
subsectors whose eligibility has been demonstrated by another EEA State and
approved by either ESA or the Commission, and to inform ESA of any such
inclusions.’
* These criteria are a trade intensity above 20 % and an indirect emission
intensity above 0.32 kg CO2/EUR, resulting in an indirect carbon leakage
indicator above 0.064. The methodology is described in further detail in
Commission staff working document SWD(2020) 190 final.
3. Point (27) is replaced by the following:
‘The aid is proportionate and has a sufficiently limited negative effect on
competition and trade if it does not exceed 80 % of the indirect emission costs
incurred for the sectors listed in Table 1 of Annex I and 75 % for the sectors
listed in Table 2 of Annex I or any further sectors considered eligible pursuant
to the procedure set out in point (21). The electricity consumption efficiency
benchmark ensures that support to inefficient production processes remains
limited and maintains the incentive for dissemination of most energy-efficient
technologies.’
4. Point (31) is replaced by the following:
‘Given that for some sectors the aid intensity of 80 % for the sectors listed in
Table 1 of Annex I and 75 % for the sectors listed in Table 2 of Annex I or any
further sectors considered eligible pursuant to the procedure set out in point (21)
might not be sufficient to ensure that there is adequate protection against the
risk of carbon leakage, when needed, EEA EFTA States may limit the amount
of the indirect costs to be paid at undertaking level to 1.5 % of the gross value
added of the undertaking concerned in year t. The gross value added of the
undertaking must be calculated as turnover, plus capitalised production, plus
other operating income, plus or minus changes in stocks, minus purchases of
goods and services (which shall not include personnel costs), minus other taxes
on products that are linked to turnover but not deductible, minus duties and
taxes linked to production. Alternatively, it can be calculated from gross
operating surplus by adding personnel costs. Income and expenditure classified
as financial or extraordinary in company accounts is excluded from value added.
Value added at factor costs is calculated at gross level, as value adjustments
(such as depreciation) are not subtracted (*).

* Code 12 15 0 within the legal framework set out by Regulation (EC) No
295/2008 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 11 March 2008
concerning structural business statistics (OJ L 97, 9.4.2008, p. 13).’
5. Point (55)(a) is replaced by the following:
‘(a) implement recommendations of the audit report, to the extent that the pay-
back time for the relevant investments does not exceed 3 years or the pay-back
time referred to in Article 10a(1), 3rd subparagraph of Directive 2003/87/EC,
whichever is longer, and that the costs of their investments is proportionate; or
alternatively’.
6. Point (55)(c) is replaced by the following:
‘(c) invest a significant share of at least 50 % of the aid amount in projects that
lead to substantial reductions of the installation’s greenhouse gas emissions
and well below the applicable benchmark used for free allocation in the EU
Emissions Trading System; or alternatively’.
7. In point (55) a new sub-point (d) is added after sub-point (c):
‘(d) invest at least 50 % of the aid amount in new or modernised assets that can
be measurably shown to make an additional contribution to reducing the costs
of the electricity system, reflecting market and system needs in that EEA EFTA
State, without resulting in an increase of fossil fuel consumption. Eligible
investment activities can include, for example, the development of renewable
energy generation capacities, energy storage solutions, measures to increase
demand-side flexibility, energy efficiency improvements that impact electricity
demand, and the development of electrolysers for the production of renewable
or low-carbon hydrogen. Investments aimed at electrification are also eligible.
EEA EFTA States may establish a more limited list of eligible investments, but
