Josephides.

title: Case 95334: College Decision 051/26/COL - State aid - Norway - One-year prolongation and amendments to the Norwegian tax refund scheme for employing seafarers
text:
Brussels, 27 February 2026
Case No: 95334
Document No: 1585810
Decision No 051/26/COL
Ministry of Trade, Industry and Fisheries
PO Box 8090
Dep 0032 Oslo
Norway
Subject: Prolongation of and amendment to the tax and social
security contributions refund scheme for employing
seafarers
1 Summary
(1) The EFTA Surveillance Authority (“ESA”) wishes to inform Norway that, having
assessed the notified one-year prolongation of the tax and social security
contributions refund scheme for employing seafarers (“the tax refund scheme”) and
the notified amendment to that scheme (“the measure”), it considers that the
measure constitutes State aid within the meaning of Article 61(1) of the EEA
Agreement and decides not to raise objections1 to the measure, as it is compatible
with the functioning of the EEA Agreement, pursuant to its Article 61(3)(c). ESA has
based its decision on the following considerations.
2 Procedure
(2) The Norwegian authorities notified the measure on 26 January 2026.2
3 Description of the measure
3.1 Background
(3) On 27 April 2016, ESA approved the tax refund scheme for employing seafarers
2016-2026 (“the original decision”).3 In 2017 and 2018, ESA approved
amendments to the tax refund scheme for employing seafarers,4 and the scheme
was also subject to three temporary amendments due to the COVID-19 outbreak
(“the amendment decisions”).5
1 Reference is made to Article 4(3) of Part II of Protocol 3 to the Agreement between the EFTA
States on the Establishment of a Surveillance Authority and a Court of Justice.
2 Document No 1586724, Document No 1586718, Document No 1586720 and Document No
1586722.
3 ESA Decision No 085/16/COL, The tax refund scheme for employing seafarers 2016-2026.
4 ESA Decision No 156/17/COL, Amendment to the tax and social security contributions refund
scheme for employing seafarers 2016-2026 (NIS deep sea), and ESA Decision No 043/18/COL,
amendment to the tax and social security contributions refund scheme for employing seafarers
2016- 2026 (NIS ferries in foreign trade).
5 ESA Decision No 088/20/COL, temporary amendments to the tax refund scheme for employing
seafarers, ESA Decision No 011/21/COL, renewal of the amendment to the tax refund scheme for
employing seafarers and ESA Decision No 218/21/COL, 2nd renewal of the temporary amendment
to the tax refund scheme for employing seafarers.
Avenue des Arts 19H, B-1000 Brussels, tel: +32 2 286 18 11, www.eftasurv.int

Page 2
(4) The tax refund scheme as approved by the original decision introduced seven
categories covering ships in both the Norwegian Ordinary Register (“NOR”) and the
Norwegian International Ship Register (“NIS”) performing various trades, with
varying eligibility criteria.6
(5) The scheme was subsequently amended, as approved by amendment decisions in
2017 and 2018.7 The 2017 amendment decision allowed for the creation of a new
category for vessels registered in the NIS engaged in deep-sea activities.8 The
2018 amendment decision allowed for the introduction of a new category, namely
passenger vessels registered in the NIS providing regular transport services
between Norwegian and foreign ports (NIS ferries in foreign trade).9 Although the
2018 amendment decision allowed for the creation of this new category, the
Norwegian authorities did not implement the model, and consequently, no aid has
been granted under this provision.
(6) Temporary amendments to the tax refund scheme were made in response to the
outbreak of COVID-19, as approved by further amendment decisions in 2020 and
2021.10 The amendments temporarily removed the ceilings for refunds for the
offshore shipping category in the NOR, the construction vessel category in the NIS
and the general category for vessels registered in the NIS.
(7) In 2025, ESA approved amendments which included incremental increases in the
maximum aid amount per seafarer from NOK 208 000 in 2017 to NOK 225 500 in
2025 for the offshore shipping categories for ships registered in the NOR and the
NIS and introduced annual aid ceilings per seafarer across various categories.11
(8) As explained in the original decision,12 the maritime transport sector is subject to
fierce international competition. According to the Norwegian authorities, several
third countries have developed significant open shipping registers, often supported
by an efficient international services infrastructure, attracting shipowners through a
fiscal climate considerably more favourable than within EEA States. Furthermore,
the low-tax environment has resulted in incentives for shipping companies to flag
their vessels outside the EEA States, without use of EEA crew, and to consider
corporate relocation. Cost considerations, including labour costs, create incentives
6 ESA Decision No 085/16/COL, The tax refund scheme for employing seafarers 2016-2026,
paragraph 14.
7 ESA Decision No 156/17/COL, Amendment to the tax and social security contributions refund
scheme for employing seafarers 2016-2026 (NIS deep sea), and ESA Decision No 043/18/COL,
amendment to the tax and social security contributions refund scheme for employing seafarers
2016- 2026 (NIS ferries in foreign trade).
8 ESA Decision No 156/17/COL, Amendment to the tax and social security contributions refund
scheme for employing seafarers 2016-2026 (NIS deep sea), paragraphs 5-8.
9 ESA Decision No 043/18/COL, amendment to the tax and social security contributions refund
scheme for employing seafarers 2016- 2026 (NIS ferries in foreign trade), paragraphs 6-11.
10 ESA Decision No 088/20/COL, Temporary amendments to the tax refund scheme for employing
seafarers, paragraphs 6-9, ESA Decision No 011/21/COL, renewal of the amendment to the tax
refund scheme for employing seafarers, paragraph 9 and ESA Decision No 218/21/COL, 2nd
renewal of the temporary amendment to the tax refund scheme for employing seafarers, paragraph
10.
11 ESA Decision No 066/25/COL, Amendments to the Norwegian tax refund scheme for employing
seafarers, paragraph 10.
12 ESA Decision No 085/16/COL, The tax refund scheme for employing seafarers 2016-2026,
paragraphs 3-5.

Page 3
for shipping companies both to flag their vessels outside the EEA States and to
recruit crews from outside the EEA.
(9) As reflected in ESA’s Maritime Guidelines (“the Maritime Guidelines”),13 there are
no effective international rules at present to curb such tax competition and few
administrative legal or technical barriers preventing withdrawal of a ship’s
registration from Norwegian or EEA registers. According to the Maritime Guidelines,
the creation of conditions allowing fairer competition with flags of convenience
therefore seems to be the best way forward.14
(10) Several EEA States have therefore introduced aid schemes for shipowners and
vessels registered in their national registers and EEA registers. Against this
backdrop, the Norwegian authorities have developed and adopted various policy
measures in order to maintain and improve the competitiveness of Norwegian-
based solutions to the alternatives abroad, mainly through the NIS register
(introduced in 1987), tax refund schemes (introduced in 1993) and the tonnage tax
(introduced in 1997).
(11) The maritime industry in Norway employs roughly 60 600 people and had a value
creation of NOK 83.2 billion in 2022. More than 200 Norwegian shipping companies
underpin a complete maritime cluster ranging from ocean transport to ship
equipment, shipbuilding, shipbroking, financing, insurance, R&D, classification and
maritime offshore oil-related activities.15
(12) An expressed objective for the Norwegian Government is to preserve the country's
position as a leading maritime nation, reflecting the industry’s vital influence in
coastal communities and its importance for maintaining and developing practical
maritime knowledge and competence. According to the Norwegian authorities, the
current geopolitical situation has strengthened the focus on retaining ships and
seafarers to ensure preparedness and resilience, and with open markets for EEA
and non-EEA competitors, it is crucial that the industry can operate on a level
playing field.
(13) As the largest flag States in the world are non-EEA States, the tax refund scheme
for seafarers, among other Norwegian policies, has enabled Norway to remain a
crucial contributor to a substantial EEA fleet and maritime workforce.
(14) Against this background, the Norwegian authorities plan to prolong the tax refund
scheme for one year and to extend it to cover eligible seafarers working on
qualifying ships registered in any EEA State. This means that shipping companies
operating vessels registered in an EEA State, and paying social contributions to the
Norwegian Maritime Pension Fund for eligible seafarers, may apply for grants under
the tax refund scheme. Norwegian and EEA seafarers working on EEA- or
Norwegian-registered vessels will qualify for refunds, enabling the shipping
company employing them to apply for grants.
13 OJ C 103, 28.4.2005, p. 24, and EEA Supplement No 21, 28.4.2005, p. 18.
14 Maritime Guidelines, Section 3.1, paragraph 1.
15 Statistics provided by the Norwegian authorities. The Norwegian authorities have indicated that
these are the latest official figures available from Statistics Norway.

Page 4
3.2 Description of the Norwegian tax refund scheme
(15) The Norwegian tax refund scheme for seafarers reimburses shipowners, partially
or wholly, for costs arising from income tax and social security contributions.
(16) The scheme, as last approved, was limited to shipping companies registered in the
NOR or the NIS. As part of the measure, the Norwegian authorities will extend the
scope of the scheme to encompass all EEA-registered vessels. The original
decision introduced general eligibility conditions.16 Additionally, the scheme is
organised in several categories that differ according to activity and ship category.
(17) The current scheme allows shipping companies registered in the NOR or the NIS
to receive a refund of taxes and social security contributions for employed
seafarers, provided that the vessels fall within certain predefined categories. The
scheme consists of eight aid categories.17
(18) These categories cover ships in both the NOR and the NIS, performing various
operations. All categories have ceilings, which means that undertakings within
these categories do not necessarily receive a full refund of income taxes and social
security contributions paid for the seafarers. As of 2025,18 the ceiling for all
categories (except the general category for vessels registered in the NIS) is NOK
225 500 per seafarer, per year. Every two month-term has a limit of NOK 37 584,
meaning that only seafarers qualifying for the limit every term will reach the annual
limit.
(19) A complete overview of the requirements to receive aid under each specific
category is found in the original decision and later amendment decisions (see
paragraphs (5) to (7)).
(20) The measure consists of (i) a one-year prolongation of the existing scheme as
authorised by ESA in the original decision and subsequent amendment decisions,19
as well as (ii) an amendment of the scheme to encompass all EEA-registered
vessels.
(21) The existing aid scheme will be prolonged for one year, from 1 March 2026 to
1 March 2027.
16 ESA Decision No 085/16/COL, The tax refund scheme for employing seafarers 2016-2026,
paragraphs 15-19.
17 ESA Decision No 085/16/COL, The tax refund scheme for employing seafarers 2016-2026,
paragraph 14, ESA Decision No 156/17/COL, Amendment to the tax and social security
contributions refund scheme for employing seafarers 2016-2026 (NIS deep sea), paragraphs 5-8,
ESA Decision No 043/18/COL, amendment to the tax and social security contributions refund
scheme for employing seafarers 2016- 2026 (NIS ferries in foreign trade), paragraphs 6-9 and ESA
Decision No 066/25/COL, paragraph 4.
18 ESA Decision No 066/25/COL, Amendments to the Norwegian tax refund scheme for employing
seafarers, paragraph 10.
19 ESA Decision No 085/16/COL, The tax refund scheme for employing seafarers 2016-2026, ESA
Decision No 156/17/COL, Amendment to the tax and social security contributions refund scheme
for employing seafarers 2016-2026 (NIS deep sea), ESA Decision No 043/18/COL, amendment to
the tax and social security contributions refund scheme for employing seafarers 2016- 2026 (NIS
ferries in foreign trade), and ESA Decision No 066/25/COL, Amendments to the Norwegian tax
refund scheme for employing seafarers.

Page 5
3.3 Amendment to the existing scheme
(22) The tax refund scheme sets out a number of general eligibility conditions. The
general conditions of the scheme are described in the original decision and the
amendment decisions (see paragraphs (3) to (7)). The general conditions for
applying to the scheme have not changed since the original decision. Additionally,
the scheme is organised in a total of nine categories that differ according to activity
and ship category. The specific categories cover ships in the NOR, the NIS and
other EEA registers performing various operations. The conditions applicable to
vessels registered in other EEA States are set out in paragraph 23.
(23) The amendment that is part of the measure concerns the introduction of a new
category of vessels under the tax refund scheme. Ships registered in other EEA
registers, and not registered in the NOR or the NIS, will have a separate category,
effective from 1 September 2026.
- The new category covers vessels engaged in activities listed in sections 8-
15, cf. section 16 of the Norwegian Regulation on subsidies for the
employment of workers at sea (“the Regulation”).20 Grants for vessels in the
EEA cover all vessels registered in an EEA register, except ships registered
in the NOR or the NIS, engaged in activities covered by sections 8-15 of the
Regulation. Vessels registered in the NOR or the NIS are therefore excluded
from this category.
- For vessels engaged in maritime activities covered by sections 11, 12 and
14 of the Regulation, grants are awarded only to eligible seafarers who are
part of the safety manning.
- Tugboats and dredgers are included in the scheme, provided that the
vessel’s transport operation is documented in the operation report. Grants
are awarded only for periods where the vessel has documented more than
50% of its activity as maritime transport.
- Grants are awarded to shipping companies for eligible seafarers, cf. section
6 of the Regulation, and for training positions, cf. section 5 of the Regulation.
- The amount of grants per eligible seafarer employed cannot exceed
NOK 37 583 per period of tax deductions, social security contributions and
employers’ social security contributions.
(24) The amendment aims to cover all EEA-registered vessels engaged in the same
activities as described in sections 8-15 of the Regulation. The general eligibility
criteria for this new category of vessels are the same as for ships registered in the
NOR and the NIS, namely criteria depending on the nature of the maritime activity
the vessels are engaged in.
(25) With the introduction of the measure, the Norwegian authorities intend to amend
the Norwegian legislation. The Norwegian authorities confirm their willingness to
amend as soon as legally possible the relevant provisions of the Norwegian law to
ensure that the scheme is also extended to all eligible vessels registered in any
other EU/EEA shipping register at the same conditions as eligible vessels
20 The Norwegian regulation on subsidies for the employment of workers at sea of 26 February 2016
No 204.

Page 6
registered in a Norwegian shipping register, in line with the amendments described
above.
(26) In this respect, the Norwegian authorities state that the amendments will be
implemented in accordance with standard procedures for legislative and regulatory
changes. The Norwegian authorities foresee that the updated regulation will enter
into force on 1 September 2026.
(27) Apart from the addition of a ninth aid category as described above and the one-year
prolongation, all other eligibility conditions of the scheme as previously approved
remain unaffected.
3.4 Objective
(28) The objective of the scheme is to support maritime employment and improve the
competitiveness of the maritime sector in the EEA.
(29) According to the Norwegian authorities, the Norwegian tax refund scheme has over
time developed into an important maritime policy instrument, ensuring a significant
number of seafarers and ships under the Norwegian flag, while aiming for a
dynamic maritime cluster and pursuing an international level playing field for
Norwegian registered vessels.
(30) The Norwegian authorities explain that the objective of the tax refund scheme is to
safeguard and promote the employment of Norwegian and EEA seafarers on
Norwegian vessels, to secure recruitment and qualified training of seafarers and to
improve the competitive position of companies employing such seafarers. The
scheme seeks to preserve and develop know-how in the maritime industries in
general and to promote safe, efficient, secure and environmentally friendly maritime
transport.
(31) According to the Norwegian authorities, the amendments to the scheme to include
all EEA-registered vessels may further increase employment opportunities for
Norwegian and other EEA seafarers and enable the ships they work aboard to
compete for market shares. The amended scheme will therefore contribute to
maritime competence for EEA seafarers and the recruitment of seafarers on EEA-
registered ships.
3.5 National legal basis and granting authority
(32) The national legal basis for the refund scheme for seafarers is the Regulation.21
(33) The aid granting authority is the Norwegian Maritime Authority
(Sjøfartsdirektoratet).
3.6 Budget and duration
(34) The Norwegian Parliament decides on the scope of the aid scheme in its annual
decisions on the State budget. According to the Norwegian authorities, the scheme
has a budget of NOK 2 480 million for 2026.
21 The Norwegian regulation on subsidies for the employment of workers at sea of 26 February 2016
No 204.

Page 7
4 Presence of State aid
4.1 Introduction
(35) Article 61(1) of the EEA Agreement reads as follows: “Save as otherwise provided
in this Agreement, any aid granted by EC Member States, EFTA States or through
State resources in any form whatsoever which distorts or threatens to distort
competition by favouring certain undertakings or the production of certain goods
shall, in so far as it affects trade between Contracting Parties, be incompatible with
the functioning of this Agreement.”
(36) The qualification of a measure as aid within the meaning of this provision requires
the following cumulative conditions to be met: (i) the measure must be granted by
the State or through State resources; (ii) it must confer an advantage on an
undertaking; (iii) favour certain undertakings (selectivity); and (iv) threaten to distort
competition and affect trade.
(37) In the original decision and the amendment decisions, ESA concluded that the
scheme for employing seafarers 2016-2026 constitutes State aid within the
meaning of Article 61(1) of the EEA Agreement.22 This one-year prolongation of the
scheme does not alter the previous assessment. Considering that the measure
amends the scheme to encompass all EEA-registered vessels, ESA finds that there
is nothing in the present case that alters the conclusions drawn in its original
decision and the amendment decisions as regards the existence of aid. The
measure therefore constitutes State aid within the meaning of Article 61(1) of the
EEA Agreement.
5 Aid scheme
(38) ESA notes that the legal basis of the tax refund scheme for employing seafarers,
i.e. the Regulation (see paragraph (32)), is an act which does not require further
implementing measures for the granting of the aid, and which identifies the
beneficiaries in a general and abstract manner, namely by reference to categories
of vessels (see paragraphs (17)-(18) and (23)).23 The aid is therefore granted on
the basis of an aid scheme.
6 Lawfulness of the aid
(39) Pursuant to Article 1(3) of Part I of Protocol 3 to the Agreement between the EFTA
States on the Establishment of a Surveillance Authority and a Court of Justice
(“Protocol 3”): “The EFTA Surveillance Authority shall be informed, in sufficient time
to enable it to submit its comments, of any plans to grant or alter aid. … The State
concerned shall not put its proposed measures into effect until the procedure has
resulted in a final decision.”
(40) The Norwegian authorities have notified the measure and have yet to let it enter
into force. They have therefore complied with the obligations under Article 1(3) of
Part I of Protocol 3.
22 ESA Decision No 085/16/COL, the tax refund scheme for employing seafarers 2016-2026,
paragraph 64.
23 See Article 1(d) of Part II of Protocol 3 to the Agreement between the EFTA States on the
Establishment of a Surveillance Authority and a Court of Justice.

Page 8
7 Compatibility of the aid
7.1 Introduction
(41) ESA can declare aid compatible with the functioning of the EEA Agreement under
its Article 61(3)(c) provided that certain compatibility conditions are fulfilled.
(42) The Maritime Guidelines describe how ESA will assess the compatibility of aid
under Article 61(3)(c) of the EEA Agreement to the maritime sector.
(43) According to the Maritime Guidelines, State aid schemes that contribute to the
consolidation of the maritime cluster established in the EEA States, that help
maintain and improve maritime know-how, and that protect and promote the
employment of EEA seafarers, may be introduced.24
(44) The Maritime Guidelines set out the conditions under which measures in the
maritime sector may be declared compatible with the functioning of the EEA
Agreement. Therefore, where a prolongation of the scheme fulfils the applicable
conditions laid down in the Maritime Guidelines, the measure will be considered
compatible with the EEA Agreement.
(45) In the original decision, ESA concluded that the tax refund scheme for employing
seafarers 2016-2026 was compatible with the functioning of the EEA Agreement
pursuant to its Article 61(3)(c).25 The objectives of the scheme, as described in
Section 3.4 above, remain unchanged. ESA considers that, taking into account the
notified one-year prolongation and the described amendments, the scheme
continues to be in line with the Maritime Guidelines.26 The measure therefore does
not affect the compatibility assessment set out in the original decision or in the
amendment decisions.
(46) ESA considers that the scheme, including the amendments notified as part of the
measure, continues to facilitate the development of certain economic activities or
regions and does not adversely affect trading conditions to an extent contrary to the
common interest, as it meets the relevant conditions of the Maritime Guidelines. In
particular, the necessity of the measure is confirmed by the finding, made in the
Maritime Guidelines, that the market alone fails to deliver an effective result in
preventing the relocation of EEA-registered vessels towards third countries where
lower regulatory standards apply.27 ESA also considers that the measure is an
appropriate instrument to lower the costs of eligible undertakings and thus enable
them to cope effectively with this international competition, in line with the Maritime
Guidelines, which state that “[S]tate aid to the EEA shipping industry is still justified”
and that tax relief measures “can generally be endorsed”.28 ESA also notes that in
recent decisions, the European Commission (“the Commission”) has confirmed that
24 Maritime Guidelines, Section 2.2, paragraph 1.
25 ESA Decision No 085/16/COL, the tax refund scheme for employing seafarers 2016-2026,
paragraphs 68-87.
26 The Maritime Guidelines apply only to “maritime transport”. Maritime transport is defined in Section
2, paragraph 3, of the Maritime Guidelines as “the transport of goods and persons by sea”. In 2016,
ESA concluded that the Maritime Guidelines should be applied by analogy to the vessels covered
by the NOR and NIS offshore shipping categories described in Sections 6.3.2 and 6.4.2 of the
Original Decision (see Original Decision, paragraphs 75-76). ESA considers that this is still the case.
27 Maritime Guidelines, Section 3.1, paragraph 1. See also paragraphs (8)-(10) above.
28 Maritime Guidelines, Section 1.2, paragraph 10, and Section 3.1, paragraph 5.

Page 9
these findings described in its equivalent version of the Maritime Guidelines are still
relevant today.29
(47) ESA positively notes the commitment of the Norwegian authorities to extend the
benefits of the scheme to all qualifying EEA-registered vessels as described in
Section 3.3 above. Following this amendment, the measure will apply to all eligible
vessels registered in any EU/EEA shipping register on the same conditions as
vessels registered in the NOR or the NIS currently covered under the scheme. The
measure therefore complies with the Maritime Guidelines in this respect.
(48) Additionally, it follows from Section 2.2, paragraph 1 of the Maritime Guidelines that
one of the objectives of State aid schemes for maritime transport is “encouraging
the flagging or re-flagging to EEA States’ registers”.
(49) The objective of the scheme, as amended by the measure, is to encourage shipping
companies to employ Norwegian and EEA seafarers and to make EEA States’
registers more attractive for Norwegian and EEA companies operating vessels
currently registered in third countries. Furthermore, the amendment aims to
preserve maritime employment and know-how in Norway by encouraging shipping
companies to employ Norwegian and EEA seafarers and to flag or re-flag their
vessels to EEA registers. In line with the Maritime Guidelines, the scheme seeks to
maintain a competitive Norwegian/EEA-flagged fleet and to strengthen the
attractiveness of EEA registers for companies currently using third-country flags.
(50) In light of the above, ESA concludes that the measure satisfies the relevant
requirements of the Maritime Guidelines and that the positive effects of the measure
outweigh the possible negative effects on competition and trade between the
Contracting Parties.30
7.2 Compliance with other provisions of EEA law
(51) If a State aid measure, the conditions attached to it (including its financing method
when the financing method forms an integral part of the State aid measure), or the
activity it finances entail a violation of relevant EEA law, the aid cannot be declared
compatible with the functioning of the EEA Agreement.31
(52) When assessing the compatibility of aid with the functioning of the EEA Agreement,
ESA must consider possible infringements of provisions of EEA law other than
those relating to State aid where such an infringement arises from the economic
activity financed, from the aid or from its object as such, or from aspects that are
inextricably linked to the object of the aid. An inextricable link exists only with regard
to aspects that are specifically necessary for the aid measure to function or to attain
its objective. Measures adopted by the EEA State concerned that are linked de
29 Commission Decision No SA.113920, Belgium – Prolongation of the aid scheme in favour of the
merchant navy, and the dredging and towing sectors, OJ C/2025/436, 20.1.2025, paragraphs 41-42
and Commission Decision No SA.111368, Italian International Registry Scheme (re-introduction of
SA.48260), OJ C/2025/4509, 11.8.2025, paragraphs 74-75, 90-91, 94 and 96.
30 See footnote 26, as regards the NOR and NIS offshore shipping categories.
31 Judgments of 19 September 2000, Germany v Commission, C-156/98, EU:C:2000:467,
paragraph 78; 22 December 2008, Régie Networks, C-333/07, EU:C:2008:764, paragraphs 94–116;
22 September 2020, Austria v Commission (Hinkley Point C), C-594/18 P, EU:C:2020:742,
paragraph 44; 14 October 2010, Nuova Agricast, C-390/06, EU:C:2008:224, paragraphs 50–51.

Page 10
facto but are legally distinct do not constitute indissociable aspects of an aid
measure.32
(53) On 24 July 2025, ESA’s Internal Market Affairs Directorate opened an own-initiative
case to investigate the compatibility with EEA law of newly adopted legislation in
Norway33 that mandates Norwegian pay to seafarers on ships in the Norwegian
territorial sea, the exclusive economic zone and on the continental shelf,
irrespective of the flag of registration. This includes sectorial legislation that
imposes a duty on licence holders in different ocean-based industries to ensure
Norwegian wages for workers on ships, irrespective of the flag of registration, that
provide services to their activities (“the Norwegian pay measures”).
(54) One of the main concerns of ESA’s Internal Market Affairs Directorate at this
preliminary stage is that this legislation might limit the freedom to provide maritime
transport services with ships registered in EEA States. The investigation is currently
ongoing.
(55) As mentioned, the objective of the tax refund scheme is to secure maritime
competence and to strengthen the recruitment of EEA seafarers. It aims to support
maritime employment and improve the competitiveness of the maritime sector in
the EEA by reducing the cost of employing EEA seafarers (see paragraphs (28)-
(31) and (49)). According to the Norwegian authorities, the objective of the
Norwegian pay measures is to protect seafarers and counteract unfair competition
in the Norwegian market, where unreasonably low wages are used to achieve a
competitive advantage.
(56) The Norwegian authorities state that the Norwegian pay measures and the tax
refund scheme for employing seafarers pursue different objectives and are, as
such, not inextricably linked to each other. The Norwegian authorities take the view
that the two sets of measures are not dependent on each other in order to function
and are therefore independent from one another.
(57) Specifically, the Norwegian authorities submit that the two measures address
different challenges. In particular, the Norwegian authorities have shown that two
thirds of coastal shipping is performed by foreign-flagged vessels, both from other
EEA States and from third countries, where seafarers may be employed on wages
below Norwegian standards. This situation persists regardless of the existence of
different State aid schemes available to EEA shipowners.
(58) Additionally, the Norwegian authorities state that the tax refund scheme for the
employment of seafarers pursues a different objective than the Norwegian pay
measures because it does not protect seafarers working in Norway against
unreasonably low wages but rather compensates businesses for the economic
disadvantage of employing EEA seafarers. The Norwegian authorities argue in this
32 Judgment of 11 September 2025, Austria v Commission, C-59/23 P, EU:C:2025:686, paragraphs
53 and 70-71, and order of 14 December 2023, CAPA and Others v Commission, C-742/21 P,
EU:C:2023:1000, paragraph 93 and the case-law cited.
33 Decision of the Norwegian Parliament of 2 June 2025: Lovvedtak 84 (2024-2025) om endringer i
allmenngjøringsloven og petroleumsloven mv. (allmenngjøringslovens anvendelse på innenriks
skipsfart og rettighetshaveres plikt til å sørge for norske lønnsvilkår på skip). (Legislative decision
84 (2024-2025) on changes to the General Application Act and Petroleum Act etc. (the application
of the General Application Act to domestic shipping and the duty of licensees to ensure Norwegian
wage conditions on ships).

Page 11
regard that State aid is not a suitable measure to protect seafarers against low
wages, or to prevent unfair low-wage competition.
(59) In ESA’s assessment, the fact that the tax refund scheme has functioned for more
than ten years without the Norwegian pay measures indicates that the scheme can
operate independently of the Norwegian pay measures.
(60) As noted above, it is clear from the case law of the Court of Justice of the European
Union that measures adopted by the EEA State concerned that are linked de facto
but are legally distinct do not constitute indissociable aspects of an aid measure.34
(61) It is ESA’s view that no such inextricable link exists in the present case. The
Norwegian pay measures do not constitute aspects that are specifically necessary
for the aid measure, i.e. the tax refund scheme, to function or to attain its objective,
but constitute rather a complementary measure. As noted, the tax refund scheme
has functioned for more than ten years without the Norwegian pay measures,
contributing to the objective of supporting maritime employment and improving the
competitiveness of the maritime sector in the EEA (see paragraphs (13), (29)-(30)
and (59)). The criteria for ESA to consider additional EEA law provisions in the
assessment of the tax refund scheme are therefore not fulfilled.
(62) In conclusion, ESA has no indications that the measure in this case, the conditions
attached to it, or the activity it finances entail a violation of relevant EEA law. This
assessment is supported by the fact that, as part of the measure, the Norwegian
authorities will extend the scope of the scheme to vessels registered in any EU/EEA
registers.
8 Conclusion
(63) On the basis of the foregoing assessment, ESA considers that the measure
constitutes State aid within the meaning of Article 61(1) of the EEA Agreement.
Since ESA has no doubts that the aid is compatible with the functioning of the EEA
Agreement pursuant to its Article 61(3)(c), it has no objections to the
implementation of the measure.
(64) This decision is without prejudice to ESA’s assessment of the compatibility of the
Norwegian pay measures with EEA law. Additionally, this decision does in no way
affect ESA’s assessment of any future amendments to the Norwegian tax refund
scheme for employing seafarers, nor the assessment of the effects of any
regulatory changes on the compatibility of that scheme with the functioning of the
EEA Agreement.
(65) The Norwegian authorities have confirmed that the notification does not contain any
business secrets or other confidential information that should not be published.
For the EFTA Surveillance Authority, acting under Delegation Decision No
068/17/COL,
34 Judgment of 11 September 2025, Austria v Commission, C-59/23 P, EU:C:2025:686, paragraph
71, and order of 14 December 2023, CAPA and Others v Commission, C-742/21 P,
EU:C:2023:1000, paragraph 93 and the case-law cited.

Page 12
Yours faithfully,
Arne Røksund
President
Responsible College Member
Melpo-Menie Joséphidès
Countersigning as Director,
Legal and Executive Affairs
This document has been electronically authenticated by Arne Roeksund, Melpo-
Menie Josephides.

title: Case 91804: College Decision 047/26/COL - Norway's derogation under Art. 49(5) of the Gas Directive
text:
Case No: 91804
Document No: 1582842
Decision No: 047/26/COL
EFTA SURVEILLANCE AUTHORITY DECISION
of 27 February 2026
on the temporary derogation for the Norwegian gas distribution system operator Lyse
Neo AS from third-party access requirements under Directive 2009/73/EC of 13 July
2009 concerning common rules for the internal market in natural gas
THE EFTA SURVEILLANCE AUTHORITY,
Having regard to:
the Agreement on the European Economic Area1 (‘the EEA Agreement’), in particular Article
24 and Annex IV thereof,
the Act referred to at point 23 of Annex IV to the EEA Agreement:
Directive 2009/73/EC of the European Parliament and of the Council of 13 July 2009
concerning common rules for the internal market in natural gas and repealing Directive
2003/55/EC (‘the Gas Directive’),2 and in particular Articles 49(4) and 49(5) thereof, as
adapted to the EEA Agreement,3
Whereas:
1 INTRODUCTION
(1) Norway is part of the internal natural gas market through the EEA Agreement. The
objective of the EEA Agreement is to create a homogeneous European Economic Area
(‘EEA’). Relevant EU legislation for the internal natural gas market is incorporated into the
EEA Agreement so that it applies throughout the whole EEA, ensuring uniform application
of laws relating to this market and thus a level playing field throughout the EEA.
(2) Following the incorporation of the Gas Directive into Annex IV to the EEA Agreement by
Decision of the EEA Joint Committee No. 93/2017 of 5 May 2017 (‘JCD No. 93/2017’),4
the requirements of the Gas Directive, as adapted to the EEA Agreement, apply to
Norway.
1 OJ L 1, 3.1.1994, p. 3.
2 OJ L 211, 14.8.2009, p. 94.
3 EEA Joint Committee Decision No. 93/2017 of 5 May 2017, in particular the adaptation set out at point
23(l) of Annex IV to the EEA Agreement.
4 EEA Joint Committee Decision No. 93/2017 of 5 May 2017 entered into force on 3 October 2019.
Avenue des Arts 19H, 1000 Brussels, tel: +32 2 286 18 11, www.eftasurv.int

Page 2
(3) EEA States are required pursuant to Article 32 of the Gas Directive to ensure objective
and non-discriminatory third-party access to the transmission and distribution systems.
(4) Articles 49(4) and 49(5) of the Gas Directive, as adapted by JCD No. 93/2017, allow,
under certain conditions, a temporary derogation to be granted from, inter alia, the third-
party access requirements where the implementation of the Gas Directive would cause
substantial problems in a geographically limited area of an EEA State, “in particular
concerning the development of the transmission and major distribution infrastructure, and
with a view to encouraging investments”.
(5) Article 49(5) of the Gas Directive, as adapted by JCD No. 93/2017, exempts two
geographically limited areas in Norway from, inter alia, Article 32 of the Gas Directive: i)
Jæren and Ryfylke, and ii) Hordaland. The derogation applies for a maximum of 20 years
after the entry into force of JCD No. 93/2017, subject to a decision by the Norwegian
energy regulatory authority (‘RME’) every five years on the need to continue the
derogation. RME is to notify the EEA Joint Committee and the EFTA Surveillance
Authority (‘the Authority’) of its decision and the assessment it is based upon.
(6) Within a period of two months from the day following the receipt of RME’s decision,5 the
Authority may adopt a decision requiring RME to amend or withdraw its decision. In such
a case, RME is to comply with the Authority’s decision within a period of one month and
to inform the EEA Joint Committee and the Authority accordingly.
(7) The present Decision follows from RME’s notification to the Authority on 3 November
2025 of its decision of the same date,6 by which RME prolonged the derogation granted
to the Norwegian gas distribution system operator (‘DSO’) in Jæren and Ryfylke, Lyse
Neo AS (‘Lyse Neo’), from the third-party access requirements under Article 32 of the Gas
Directive7 for a period of five years from 1 January 2026 (‘the RME Decision’).
(8) The RME Decision was issued following the previous decision notified by RME to the
Authority on 3 October 2024,8 which continued the derogation for Lyse Neo from third-
party access requirements under Article 32 of the Gas Directive for a shorter period from
3 October 2024 until 31 December 2025. The Authority, having identified a lack of
information and analysis to demonstrate the need for a continued derogation, issued a
decision on 19 March 2025 (‘the Authority’s Decision No. 025/25/COL’),9 which required
RME to amend its decision of 3 October 2024 by providing certain clarifications and
justifications.10 RME subsequently adopted an amending decision on 22 April 2025.11
(9) This Decision is issued following the Authority’s review of the RME Decision.
5 This period may be extended with the consent of both the Authority and RME.
6 The date of the RME Decision was corrected from 27 October 2025 to 3 November 2025 by email
dated 18 February 2025.
7 Reference is made in the RME Decision to the Norwegian provisions implementing Article 32 of the
Gas Directive, namely Section 5-1 and Chapters 3,6 and 7 of the Norwegian regulation on natural gas
no. 1342 of 14 November 2003 (FOR-2003-11-14-1342). In Norwegian: ‘Forskrift til lov om felles regler
for det indre marked for naturgass (naturgassforskriften)’.
8 Documents No. 1488105, 1488106 and 1488107.
9 EFTA Surveillance Authority Decision No. 025/25/COL of 19 March 2025 on the temporary derogation
for the Norwegian gas distribution system operator Lyse Neo AS from requirements under Directive
2009/73/EC of 13 July 2009 concerning common rules for the internal market in natural gas.
10 RME was required to: (i) clarify its reasons for granting a continued derogation for Lyse Neo from 3
October 2024 until 31 December 2025, taking into account the elements identified by the Authority as
lacking and specified in the Authority’s decision, and (ii) justify the duration of the derogation.
11 Documents No. 1532572 and 1532614.

Page 3
2 PROCEDURE
(10)On 4 July 2025, RME submitted to the Authority a preliminary decision indicating that it
planned to continue Lyse Neo’s derogation from third-party access requirements for a
new period of five years starting from 1 January 2026 (Documents No. 1548248, 1548249
and 1548250).12 RME also submitted a supporting document (Document No. 1548247).13
(11)RME submitted additional documents, which were taken into account for the preparation
of RME’s preliminary decision, to the Authority on 15 August 202514 and on 1 October
2025.15
(12)On 3 November 2025, RME notified to the Authority the RME Decision (Documents No.
1571348, 1571349 and 1571350), maintaining its preliminary position to continue Lyse
Neo’s derogation for five years from 1 January 2026.
(13)By email of 5 November 2025, the Authority invited RME to share the input which RME
had received during the public consultation on its preliminary decision (Document No.
1572006). RME submitted that input on 7 November 2025.16
(14)Several exchanges and meetings took place between the Authority and RME to clarify
the information and documents submitted by RME.17
(15)By letters of 2 and 5 December 2025, the Authority and RME agreed to extend the period
for the Authority to issue its decision until 27 February 2026 (Documents No. 1576425
and 1579061).
3 THE NATURAL GAS MARKET IN NORWAY
(16)The Norwegian domestic market for natural gas consists of two distribution systems,
respectively owned and operated by Molgas Energy, previously known as Gasnor,
(‘Molgas’) and Lyse Neo.
3.1 Lyse Neo’s gas distribution system
(17)Lyse Neo’s gas distribution system is located in Jæren and Ryfylke. This area is subject
to the present Decision.
12 Pursuant to Section 5-2, fourth paragraph of the Norwegian regulation on natural gas no. 1342 of 14
November 2003 (FOR-2003-11-14-1342). The Authority notes that the Norwegian legal framework does
not adequately reflect the procedure for Norway under Article 49(5) of the Gas Directive, as adapted by
JCD No. 93/2017. The present Decision is without prejudice to any future action the Authority may
undertake with regard to Norway’s implementation of this procedure.
13 This supporting document sets out RME’s comments related to the following matters which were not
addressed in its preliminary decision: (i) Oslo Economics' report of February 2025, (ii) explanation of
various calculations made by RME, and (iii) clarification on the decarbonisation package.
14 Report from Menon Economics of 28 February 2025 (‘Menon Economics Report’), ‘Konsekvenser av
tredjepartsadgang for biogassprodusenter og balanseringskostnader i Lyse Neo sitt gassrørnett’
(Documents No. 1554530 and 1554532).
15 Submissions to RME by Lyse Neo, including internal accounts, product accounts, yearly costs and
revenues, and tariff estimates, as well as RME calculations. Documents No. 1565503, 1565515,
1565516, 1565517, 1565518, 1565519, 1565520, 1565521, 1565522, 1565523 and 1565524.
16 Documents No. 1572525, 1572530, 1572529, 1572528, 1572527 and 1572526.
17 The Authority and RME met on 9 May 2025, 23 June 2025, 14 August 2025, 15 September 2025 and
on 8 October 2025.

Page 4
(18)According to the RME Decision, Lyse Neo is a company part of the Lyse Group, which
has activities in, inter alia, the production of renewable power, telecommunications and
distribution of electricity. Its gas distribution system was built between 2001 and 2005 and
consists of the following infrastructure:
(i) the Rogass sea pipeline of 50 km, which mainly supplies an LNG factory in Risavika,
and which has the capacity to transport 15 TWh a year; and
(ii) 600 km of land network, with a transport capacity of 2.3 TWh a year.
(19)Since the incorporation of the Gas Directive into the EEA Agreement, Lyse Neo’s
distribution system has been exempted from Articles 24, 31 and 32 of the Gas Directive,
pursuant to Article 49(5) of the Gas Directive, as adapted by JCD No. 93/2017. According
to the information provided by RME, this exemption was granted because Lyse Neo’s
system was built during the period when Norway held the status of an “emergent market”
under Directive 2003/55/EC18 from 10 April 2004 until 10 April 2014.19
(20)Following RME’s decisions of 3 October 2024 and 22 April 2025,20 and the RME Decision
subject to the present Decision, Lyse Neo currently benefits from a derogation from third-
party access requirements under Article 32 of the Gas Directive.
3.2 Molgas’ gas distribution system
(21)Molgas’ gas distribution system, located at Karmøy in Norway and built in 1994, was
opened for third-party access on 1 January 2023. The Authority understands, based on
the information provided by RME, that no third parties currently supply gas through
Molgas’ system.
4 LEGAL FRAMEWORK
(22)Article 49(4) of the Gas Directive allows a temporary derogation from, inter alia, third-party
access requirements under Article 32 of the Gas Directive where the implementation of
the Gas Directive would cause “substantial problems” in a geographically limited area, “in
particular concerning the development of the transmission and major distribution
infrastructure, and with a view to encouraging investments […] for developments within
that area”.
(23)Article 49(5) of the Gas Directive, as adapted by JCD No. 93/2017, sets out the procedure
to be applied for Norway for the continuation of such derogations (see paragraphs (5) and
(6) above), and it provides the criteria that RME must take into account for its decision,
“in particular […]:
18 Directive 2003/55/EC of the European Parliament and of the Council of 26 June 2003 concerning
common rules for the internal market in natural gas and repealing Directive 98/30/EC, OJ L 176,
15.7.2003, p. 57, as incorporated into the EEA Agreement and adapted by EEA Joint Committee
Decision No. 146/2005 of 2 December 2005.
19 Pursuant to Article 2(31) of Directive 2003/55/EC, "emergent market" means an EEA State in which
the first commercial supply of its first long-term natural gas supply contract was made not more than 10
years earlier.
20 RME did not continue the derogation from the requirement to designate Lyse Neo as a DSO under
Article 24 of the Gas Directive, since Lyse Neo was designated as a DSO from 1 February 2021, and
found that it was not necessary to continue the derogation from accounting rules under Article 31 of the
Gas Directive.

Page 5
- the need for infrastructure investments, which would not be economic to operate in a
competitive market environment,
- the level and pay-back prospects of investments required,
- the size and maturity of the gas system in the area concerned,
- the prospects for the gas market concerned,
- the geographical size and characteristics of the area or region concerned, and
socioeconomic and demographic factors”.
5 THE AUTHORITY’S ASSESSMENT
5.1 Summary of the RME Decision
(24)The RME Decision continued Lyse Neo’s derogation from the third-party access
requirements under Article 32 of the Gas Directive for five years starting from 1 January
2026. RME considered that third-party access is likely to create substantial problems for
Lyse Neo’s continued operation of the gas distribution system for the following main
reasons:
- Lyse Neo has not yet recovered the historic investments it made to establish the
distribution system, and it is uncertain whether this will happen in the future given the
current market outlook;
- it is uncertain whether future investments aimed at extending the economic lifetime of
the system by transitioning to biogas are profitable; and
- Lyse Neo’s distribution system is not connected to a transmission system.
(25)With regard to the duration of the derogation, RME noted that the factual circumstances
underlying its decision are expected to persist for the period from 2026 to 2030, and that
a five-year extension would help reduce regulatory uncertainty compared to a shorter
exemption.
(26)The RME Decision is based on information provided by Lyse Neo, a consultant report,21
and stakeholders’ input.22
(27)The Authority understands that the RME Decision assessed the criteria set out in Article
49(5) of the Gas Directive as summarised in the following.
5.1.1 The need for infrastructure investments
(28)The RME Decision distinguished between investments needed independently of third-
party access and investments needed as a result of potential third-party access.
(29)Regarding the first category of investments, RME noted that Lyse Neo:
21 Menon Economics Report.
22 On 4 July 2025, RME issued a notice of exemption for third-party access to Lyse Neo's gas distribution
system in Jæren and Ryfylke with a deadline for comments by 15 August 2025. RME received in total
5 responses: four from biogas producers and one from an interest organisation of which three of the
biogas producers are members. RME also received input from several stakeholders in relation to its
decision of 3 October 2024, which it still considered as relevant.

Page 6
- is facing investments in gas meters due to the shutdown of the 2G network, estimated
at 12.2 million NOK, and potentially also in storage capacity to ensure the possibility
of balancing the distribution system if the sales volume continues to fall;
- plans to replace all natural gas in the system with biogas in the long term, which will
require further investments in storage capacity for balancing purposes; and
- presented three different scenarios for the investments needed for storage capacity
for scaling up biogas production, with estimates ranging from 55 million NOK to 1 220
million NOK.
(30)Regarding the second category of investments, RME noted that:
- facilitating third-party access, according to Lyse Neo, will require additional
investments to replace gas meters, estimated at 14.4 million NOK;23 and
- investments for adjustment in the systems for settlement and invoicing will be
required, estimated at 10 million NOK.
5.1.2 The level and pay-back prospects of investments required
(31)RME noted that the high prices for natural gas in recent years led to a loss of customers
and decline in sales volumes, reducing the operating profit for Lyse Neo.24 Therefore,
RME considered that there is great uncertainty associated with the economic lifetime of
Lyse Neo’s distribution system, making it difficult for Lyse Neo to recover both historic and
future investments, even with the derogation from third-party access requirements.
(32)With regard to the historic investments made to establish the distribution system, RME
noted that:
- Lyse Neo has not yet fully recovered its investments of 1 116.6 million NOK made to
establish the distribution system;25 and
- it is uncertain based on the current market outlook whether it is possible for Lyse Neo
to obtain a reasonable compensation for the tied-up capital associated with the historic
investments.26
(33)With regard to future investments, RME noted that:
- the profitability depends on whether and when Lyse Neo’s system is opened for third-
party access;
- opening the distribution system for third-party access would reduce profitability and
could hinder investments that may be necessary to extend the economic lifetime of
the distribution system;
- although Lyse Neo could pass these costs on to third-party actors via the tariff, the
tariff for third-party undertakings will be higher than the tariff determined for Molgas
and it is therefore doubtful whether other undertakings will be interested in supplying
gas through Lyse Neo’s system;
23 Because third-party access requires remote reading for all customers to calculate what the individual
supplier delivers to different customers over hours and/or days.
24 Because the costs of operating the grid have to be distributed among fewer customers / lower
volumes. In the period 2021-2024, Lyse Neo experienced a 50 per cent reduction in the average
contribution compared with the period 2013-2016. Its operating profit was reduced considerably over
the period, from 56 million NOK on average in the period 2013-2016 to -6 million NOK in 2021-2024.
25 Based on new figures from Lyse Neo for the period 2003-2024.
26 Based on figures for turnover, operating costs, depreciation and investments in the period 2003-2024,
RME found that the net present value of the total investments in the gas pipeline network up to 2024
has been NOK -208.1 million.

Page 7
- no third-party undertakings have yet requested access to Molgas' system.
(34)RME concluded that, if Lyse Neo’s distribution system is opened for third-party access,
there is a risk of incurring additional costs for Lyse Neo (and indirectly also for its
customers) to facilitate third-party access, without having a positive effect on the
competitive situation in the distribution system.
5.1.3 The size and maturity of the gas system in the area concerned
(35)RME noted that granting third-party access to Lyse Neo’s gas distribution system would
lead to significant difficulties with regard to balancing, given that this system is not
connected to a transmission system. In this context, Lyse Neo as a DSO has the sole
responsibility for balancing the system.
5.1.4 The prospects for the gas market concerned
(36)RME noted that:
- the sales volumes of natural gas in recent years have declined,27 due to recent high
prices of natural gas, resulting in customers reducing their energy consumption and /
or converting to alternative forms of energy, including propane and electricity; and that
- the market outlook is uncertain, both with regard to the price of natural gas and of
other forms of energy as well as regulatory factors.28
(37)With regard to the market for biogas, RME further noted that:
- it is immature and not yet competitive with other forms of energy on market terms;
- although Norway has a number of support schemes to promote biogas production,
regulatory and raw market supply chains remain uncertain;
- few customers, including the Lyse Group itself, buy biogas in Lyse Neo’s distribution
system;
- Lyse Neo seeks to replace all natural gas in the system with biogas in the long term,
and considers that third-party access plays an important role in this regard; and that
- several biogas companies with investment plans in the area may be interested in
supplying biogas through Lyse Neo’s distribution system.
5.1.5 The geographical size and characteristics of the area concerned, and socioeconomic
and demographic factors
(38)RME noted that the following features reinforce Lyse Neo’s difficulties in recovering
investments with third-party access:
- Lyse Neo’s distribution system is geographically small compared to gas systems in
EU Member States, but relatively large in relation to transported volume, which
reduces Lyse Neo’s operating profit; and that
- Lyse Neo’s customers are price-sensitive and may easily switch to alternative energy
sources such as propane and electricity.
5.1.6 Other elements
27 In the period 2017-2024, Lyse Neo experienced a 40 per cent fall in sales volumes.
28 Including the CO tax, which RME considered is likely to further increase, and regulations limiting the
2
use of natural gas.

Page 8
(39)In addition to the above criteria set out in Article 49(5) of the Gas Directive, RME
considered subsequent legal developments in the EU, in particular Directive 2024/1788
of the European Parliament and of the Council of 13 June 2024 on common rules for the
internal markets in renewable gas, natural gas and hydrogen (‘Directive 2024/1788’).29
(40)RME noted that Directive 2024/1788 allows EU Member States to exempt gas distribution
systems that are not connected to other countries from third-party access requirements,
reflecting the challenges of such access for isolated systems, such as Lyse Neo’s
distribution system which is not connected to a transmission system.
5.1.7 Stakeholders’ input
(41)Finally, the RME Decision summarised the stakeholders’ input received during the public
consultation on RME’s preliminary decision, including the stakeholders’ input received in
relation to RME’s previous decision of 3 October 2024. The majority of these stakeholders
did not support the continuation of Lyse Neo’s derogation. They argued that:
- RME placed excessive emphasis on the recovery of historic investments;
- RME adopted an overly pessimistic market outlook;
- RME overstated the challenges and costs of balancing;
- the absence of third-party access would hamper the development of the biogas
industry in the area; and that
- the Norwegian regulation on natural gas30 does not permit consideration of whether
historic investments have been recovered when assessing the need for a continued
derogation, thus the legal requirements were not met.
(42)Regarding the relevance of historic investments, the RME Decision considered that:
- while return on Lyse Neo’s original investment is not explicitly listed among the
relevant criteria in the Norwegian regulation on natural gas, the reference therein to
“substantial problems […] for the development of and investment in the system”31 and
the purpose of the derogation to ensure a reasonable return for the regulated natural
monopoly allows for an assessment of whether Lyse Neo can recover the investments
necessary to establish the gas distribution system; and that
- failing to assess historic investments could undermine investment incentives.
5.2 Assessment of the legal requirements
(43)At the outset, the Authority notes that granting a derogation, being an exemption from the
rules of the Gas Directive, must be limited to what is necessary for achieving the objective
pursued, and its duration must be as short as possible. This is supported by settled case
law, from which it follows that exceptions are to be interpreted strictly so that general rules
are not negated.32
29 OJ L, 2024/1788, 15.7.2024, not yet incorporated into the EEA Agreement.
30 Section 5-2, third paragraph.
31 The Authority’s translation of Section 5-2, third paragraph, of the Norwegian regulation on natural
gas, which in Norwegian reads: ‘betydelige problemer, særlig for utvikling av og investeringer i
systemet’.
32 Compare judgment of the Court of Justice of 28 October 2022, Generalstaatsanwaltschaft München,
C-435/22 PPU, EU:C:2022:852, para. 120 and the case law cited.

Page 9
(44)The Authority, stressing that the burden to adequately demonstrate that Lyse Neo fulfils
the requirements for a continued derogation is upon RME, has the following remarks.
5.2.1 Assessment of the requirements in Article 49(4) of the Gas Directive
(45)The Authority observes that the purpose of the derogation under Article 49(4) of the Gas
Directive is to avoid “substantial problems” in a geographically limited area which may be
caused by the implementation of the Gas Directive, “in particular concerning the
development of the transmission and major distribution infrastructure, and with a view to
encouraging investments […] for developments within that area”. For example, a
derogation from third-party access requirements may encourage investments and thus
the development of infrastructure, in accordance with Article 49(5) of the Gas Directive.
(46)The Authority notes that the RME Decision concluded that opening the gas distribution
system to third-party access would likely create substantial problems for Lyse Neo’s
continued operation of the distribution system and, as such, access would reduce the
profitability of Lyse Neo’s investments which, in turn, may affect whether the investments
outlined by Lyse Neo are carried out. The Authority further understands that RME
considered that Lyse Neo's profitability in relation to both historical and future investments
must be prioritised over opening the market.
(47)While Articles 49(4) and 49(5) of the Gas Directive, read in conjunction with JCD No.
93/2017, leave discretion to RME with regards to the assessment of whether third-party
access pursuant to Article 32 of that Directive would cause ‘substantial problems’ in Jæren
and Ryfylke, the Authority observes, concerning the relevance of the historic investments
made by Lyse Neo when the distribution system was established, that:
- the requirements for granting a derogation under Articles 49(4) and 49(5) of the Gas
Directive appears to be forward-looking in nature and do not explicitly refer to such
historic investments;
- no specific adaptations to these requirements were made for Norway by JCD No.
93/2017 when granting the initial derogation for Lyse Neo’s gas distribution system,
which was already established at the time of adoption of JCD No. 93/2017; and that
- the criteria listed in Article 49(5) of the Gas Directive are not exhaustive.
(48)The Authority notes, however, that the RME Decision lacks clarity regarding RME’s
consideration of some of the criteria listed in Article 49(5) of the Gas Directive and their
implication for the assessment as to whether third-party access would cause substantial
problems as required under Article 49(4) of that Directive, as further detailed in Section
5.2.2 below.
5.2.2 Assessment of the criteria listed in Article 49(5) of the Gas Directive
(49)Firstly, with regard to “the need for infrastructure investments” and whether these “would
not be economic to operate in a competitive market environment”, in accordance with the
first criterion listed in Article 49(5) of the Gas Directive (the Authority’s emphasis), RME
noted several uncertainties as regards market and regulatory developments. The
Authority notes, however, that the RME Decision does not clearly explain whether RME
considers the investments outlined by Lyse Neo33 to be needed in light of the market
outlook.
33 The RME Decision uses different wording in this respect, referring to Lyse Neo as both “investigating”
and “planning to” undertake certain investments, which raises uncertainty as to whether those

Page 10
(50)Secondly, the Authority considers that the RME Decision does not clearly explain whether
Lyse Neo would be able to recover its investment costs through tariffs in case of third-
party access, with reference to “the level and pay-back prospects of investments required”
in the second criteria listed in Article 49(5) of the Gas Directive (the Authority’s emphasis).
The Authority notes, in this regard, that regulated tariffs allow a margin to recover costs
and that third-party access does not necessarily exclude profitability.
(51)While RME considered that it would be less profitable for Lyse Neo to undertake the
relevant investments if the distribution system were opened for third-party access, it did
not substantiate these considerations. The Authority further notes that RME considered it
doubtful whether other actors would be interested in supplying gas through Lyse Neo’s
system, inter alia because the tariffs estimated for Lyse Neo would be higher than those
for Molgas. However, in light of the number of stakeholders (e.g., biogas producers) who
expressed an interest in accessing Lyse Neo’s distribution system during the public
consultation on RME’s preliminary decision, the RME Decision does not fully explain
these considerations. The RME Decision also lacks internal consistency: if no, or only a
limited number of, third-party actors are expected to seek access, this assumption should
be reflected in RME’s assessment of the investment needs and pay-back prospects.
(52)Further to the above, the Authority notes that the RME Decision does not fully address
the elements highlighted by the Authority as lacking in its Decision No. 025/25/COL. Given
RME’s commitment to address these, inter alia through commissioning a consultant’s
report,34 the Authority invites RME to reflect, where relevant, its findings in the RME
Decision to clarify its considerations and to enhance transparency.
(53)Finally, regarding the duration of the continued derogation, the Authority notes that RME
expects the factual circumstances underlying its decision to persist for the period from
2026 to 2030. In light of the comments above, RME is invited to update, as relevant, its
assessment of the length of the derogation, noting that the derogation must be limited to
what is necessary for achieving the objective pursued, which entails that the duration must
be as short as possible (see paragraph (43) above).
(54)Due to the lack of clarity on the points listed above, the Authority considers it necessary
that RME clarifies its considerations for granting Lyse Neo a continued derogation.
5.2.3 Other elements
(55)With regard to the consideration in the RME Decision of Directive 2024/1788, the Authority
acknowledges the rationale underlying the derogation, namely, the challenges associated
with third-party access for non-connected systems, and its relevance for RME’s
assessment. However, the Authority notes that Directive 2024/1788 is not yet
incorporated into the EEA Agreement. The present Decision is based exclusively on the
requirements of the Gas Directive as currently applicable and in force under the EEA
Agreement.
5.2.4 Stakeholders’ input
investments are considered “required” and will be undertaken. In addition, it does not specify whether,
and if so which, of the estimates presented by Lyse Neo are considered the most realistic.
34 In its amended decision of 22 April 2024, RME acknowledged areas of uncertainty and indicated that
those elements would be subject to further investigations before the adoption by RME of a decision for
the period from 1 January 2026 onwards. RME therefore commissioned the Menon Economics Report.

Page 11
(56)The Authority notes that the RME Decision did not fully address the concerns raised by
stakeholders opposing the continuation of Lyse Neo’s derogation. Further to its comments
above in Section 5.2.2, and with a view to enhancing transparency, the Authority invites
RME to clarify its considerations, including, where relevant, by addressing stakeholders’
concerns.
6 CONCLUSIONS
(57)For the reasons set out above, the Authority considers that the amendments detailed in
Section 5.2.2 are necessary to ensure that the derogation granted to Lyse Neo is in line
with the requirements and the objectives of the Gas Directive.
(58)In accordance with the procedure for Norway under Article 49(5) of the Gas Directive, as
adapted by JCD No. 93/2017, the Authority requires RME to amend the RME Decision
within one month following receipt of this Decision, and to inform the Authority accordingly.
HAS ADOPTED THIS DECISION:
1. This Decision is addressed to RME.
2. This Decision enters into force upon notification to RME.
3. RME is to comply with this Decision within a period of one month and to inform the
EEA Joint Committee and the EFTA Surveillance Authority accordingly.
4. This Decision is authentic in the English language.
Done at Brussels,
For the EFTA Surveillance Authority
Arne Røksund Árni Páll Árnason Nuscha Wieczorek
President Responsible College Member College Member
Melpo-Menie Joséphidès
Countersigning as Director,
Legal and Executive Affairs
This document has been electronically authenticated by Arne Roeksund, Melpo-Menie
Josephides.

title: Case 95327: College Decision 046/26/COL - State aid - Norway - Aid scheme for broadband rollout
text:
Brussels, 26 February 2026
Case No: 95327
Document No: 1537072
Decision No 046/26/COL
Ministry of Trade, Industry and Fisheries
PO Box 8090
Dep 0032 Oslo
Norway
Subject: Aid scheme for broadband rollout in Norway
1 Summary
(1) The EFTA Surveillance Authority (“ESA”) wishes to inform Norway that, having
assessed the aid scheme for broadband rollout in Norway (“the measure”), it
considers that it constitutes State aid within the meaning of Article 61(1) of the EEA
Agreement and decides not to raise objections1 to the measure, as it is compatible
with the functioning of the EEA Agreement, pursuant to its Article 61(3)(c). ESA has
based its decision on the following considerations.
2 Procedure
(2) Following pre-notification contacts, the Norwegian authorities notified the measure
on 23 January 2026.2
(3) On 27 and 29 January 2026,3 ESA requested information from the Norwegian
authorities. On 28 and 30 January 2026,4 the Norwegian authorities replied to the
information requests.
3 Context

networks providing a download speed of at least 30 Mbit/s. The main focus was the

(6) From 2018 to 2025, the national State aid scheme for broadband rollout was based

download speed of at least 30 Mbit/s, the speed threshold being increased in 2023

3.2 Broadband availability in Norway
3.2.1 Fixed broadband
(7) The Norwegian authorities consider that overall, the broadband availability in
Norway is good. According to the Norwegian authorities’ estimates,6 approximately
99.1% of all households have access to connections providing 100 Mbit/s download
speed, while 96.2% of all households have access to gigabit connections.7 Hence,
3.8% of Norwegian households are lacking access to gigabit networks, while 0.9%
are lacking access to networks providing 100 Mbit/s.
(8) The estimates on broadband availability provided by the Norwegian authorities are
based on homes passed (“HP”). For a household to be considered as HP, the
offered connection should be available for a connection fee no higher than NOK
10 000 and the connection to the network should be provided within reasonable
time. Further, the coverage figures are calculated based on all ground-based fixed
solutions, including mobile solutions provided as fixed wireless access (“FWA”).8
3.2.1.1 FTTP- and HFC-solutions
(9) The gigabit coverage of 96.2% consists of Fibre to the Premises (“FTTP”)9 and
Hybrid Fibre-Coaxial (“HFC”)10 solutions. Most FTTP and HFC networks provide
gigabit connectivity, while the rest is considered to be upgradeable on the basis of
marginal investments to provide gigabit connectivity.
3.2.1.2 Copper network
(10) The copper network in Norway was fully decommissioned in September 2025 and
is therefore not a part of the broadband network in Norway.
3.2.1.3 Satellite-based solutions
(11) GEO satellites11 are considered as the “last option” due to poor coverage (resulting
from a low inclination in combination with a demanding topography), low bandwidth

Still, LEO satellites are at the current stage not considered to provide reliable

3.2.1.4 Mobile-based FWA solutions
(12) FWA solutions based on mobile frequencies deliver speeds in the range from 10 to
100 Mbit/s based on high-band 4G and in the range from 100 to 500 Mbit/s based
on high-band 5G. These solutions provide competition to FTTP-based and HFC-
based solutions in the market and reliable connectivity in areas where no FTTP-
based and HFC-based solutions exist. In areas lacking FTTP and HFC, mobile-
based FWA solutions are the most commonly used broadband connection, but with
a high variation in the provided top speed, ranging from 10 Mbit/s to 500 Mbit/s.
3.2.1.5 Other FWA solutions
(13) Except for mobile-based solutions, the use of FWA in Norway is limited and mainly
based on license-free frequencies. The range and throughput are limited with top
download speeds at around 100 Mbit/s.
3.2.2 Mobile broadband
(14) Norway has overall a high mobile coverage, both in terms of basic area coverage
and available upload and download speeds. Still, due to a demanding topography,
some households, socio-economic drivers and important transport routes lack
mobile coverage.
(15) Mobile coverage in Norway is based on 2G, 4G and 5G13 networks. Mobile
networks based on 3G were decommissioned in Norway in 2020. 2G networks are
expected to be decommissioned by the end of 2027.
(16) Currently, 99.7% of households in Norway have access to at least basic 5G
coverage and almost 100% have access to basic 4G or 5G coverage.14 However,
98.4% of households are covered by 4G or 5G mobile networks with at least 30
Mbit/s download speed, whilst 83.7% are covered with at least 100 Mbit/s. The
numbers vary between regions, with the most northern counties being 5% behind
the national average. There is also a considerable difference between urban and
rural areas, with rural areas having 91.1% and 43% coverage of 30 Mbit/s and
100 Mbit/s, respectively.
(17) The importance of mobile broadband coverage is not limited to households, but to
general geographic areas - especially along important transport routes. A study15
of mobile coverage conducted by Analysys Mason16 in 2022 for the Norwegian
Ministry of Local Government and Regional Development found a significant

3.3 The Norwegian national digitalisation strategy
(18) In 2024, the Norwegian government launched a new national digitalisation strategy,
“The Digital Norway of the future”, setting out the direction for digital Norway
towards 2030.
(19) This strategy set out the goal that all households and businesses should have
access to broadband with a download speed of at least 100 Mbit/s by the end of
2025 and at least 1 Gbit/s by the end of 2030. Further, high-speed mobile coverage
should be available to households. The Norwegian authorities consider that access
to high-speed mobile networks is important not only where people live, but also
where people work and travel. The goal of providing high-speed mobile coverage
to everyone and gigabit connectivity to all end-users at a fixed location by 2030 is
also in line with targets set in Decision (EU) 2022/2481 of the European Parliament
and of the Council of 14 December 2022 establishing the Digital Decade Policy
Programme 2030.20
(20) The Norwegian authorities consider that mobile and broadband networks are a
central part of Norway’s digital infrastructure and are prerequisites for further
digitalisation of society. The Norwegian authorities take the view that as society is
becoming increasingly dependent on these networks, it is necessary to
continuously improve the digital infrastructure.
(21) According to the Norwegian authorities, new services and applications based on
fibre and 5G mobile technology are rapidly being established. They provide the
basis for new ways of solving tasks and increased value creation and productivity.
The Norwegian authorities consider it important that these services are made
available also in sparsely populated areas. One hundred percent coverage is
primarily important for individual citizens to be able to participate in the society, but
it is also important for the development of businesses and for the public sector to
maximise the benefits of digitalisation. The Confederation of Norwegian Enterprise
(NHO) has published a study on the general societal use of expanding gigabit
connectivity based on fixed broadband.21 According to the Norwegian authorities,
the report gives useful indications as to the positive effects of increasing available
broadband speeds to end users.

(22) Norway has achieved a high degree of digitalisation, as illustrated in the Digital
Economy and Society Index (DESI) report.22 According to the Norwegian
authorities, this means that there is an increased digital divide as well as an
increased barrier to participate in society for those lacking modern internet
connectivity.
(23) The Norwegian authorities consider that participation and use of modern services
are important where people live and for businesses, but should also be available
where people work, travel and socialise. Thus, it is important to have a stable
connection also along major transport routes for large amounts of private travel and
transportation of goods and services.
(24) As regards mobile connectivity, end users generally require a low latency and
reliable connection, though the exact needs may vary depending on the relevant
area. According to the Norwegian authorities, network speed is of particular interest,
as modern services and applications involve a higher requirement for download and
upload speeds with low latency to be utilised effectively by end users. The
Norwegian authorities take the view that it is therefore necessary to increase the
area coverage of mobile networks to increase the available speeds and introduce
new services to end users.
(25) The Norwegian authorities consider that a reliable and nationwide mobile
connectivity is also necessary for the ability to contact emergency services, such
as police, fire and rescue and ambulance services. For end users to be able to
contact emergency services, at least one operator needs coverage in the area.
(26) Furthermore, emergency services use a dedicated network, Nødnett,23 in their
operations, separate from the generally available network to normal end users.
Currently, Nødnett is a dedicated network with basic services separate from the
commercial networks. However, according to the Norwegian authorities, a major
modernisation of Nødnett is currently ongoing, as a result of which Nødnett will be
provided through the commercial 4G and 5G networks and will take advantage of
enhanced services. As such, it will directly benefit from investments in mobile
coverage under this scheme and enable emergency services to utilise modern
mobile capabilities in their operations in areas which currently lack coverage.
4 Description of the measure
4.1 Objective
(27) The main objective of the measure is to improve the overall broadband and mobile
coverage in Norway and reduce the digital divide across areas.
(28) The measure will incentivise the rollout of broadband in geographical areas where
there is an insufficient commercial basis for investments. The aim is to contribute
to providing access to ultra-fast broadband networks to all households and
businesses, and high-speed mobile coverage to all households and businesses,
and along important transport routes. The measure will thus bring significant new
capabilities to the market in terms of broadband and mobile service availability and
